introduction.title: Dubai Police Design System 2.0
introduction.description: A shared component library, foundations, and patterns for every Dubai Police product team.

whatIsThis.title: What is this?
whatIsThis.p1: DPDS 2.0 is a shadcn-style component library and pattern catalogue for Dubai Police's internal product surfaces. Every screen built by a Dubai Police product team should compose from these primitives — not roll its own buttons, forms, or layout shells.
whatIsThis.p2: It is the single source of truth for UI primitives, design tokens, and proven product patterns. Components ship with documentation, accessibility notes, and copy-pasteable code. Tokens drive every visual decision so theming, dark mode, and RTL stay consistent across products.
whatIsThis.p3: Treat it like an internal package: import, compose, ship. Open a PR if a primitive is missing — don't fork it locally.

whoItsFor.title: Who it's for
whoItsFor.p1: Product engineers building screens inside Dubai Police apps. It is not for external customers, not for marketing surfaces, and not for one-off prototypes that won't ship.

foundations.title: Foundations
foundations.body: Colors, typography, spacing, radius, motion, elevation, iconography, and accessibility primitives.

components.title: Components
components.body: Buttons, inputs, layout, data display, navigation, feedback, and overlay primitives — composed from the foundations.

patterns.title: Patterns
patterns.body: Real, opinionated screens (Login, Sign up, dashboards) assembled from the components, ready to drop into a product.

items.scaffolds: Full application scaffolds or feature implementations — those live in each product team's repo.
items.marketing: Marketing pages, landing pages, or campaign sites — those use a separate brand system.
items.customer: Customer-facing branded portals that need their own visual identity — fork the tokens, don't extend this system.

darkMode.title: Dark mode
darkMode.description: How light, dark, and system themes work in DPDS 2.0 — and how to wire the toggle into your app.

howItWorks.title: How it works
howItWorks.p1: A .dark class on <html> (added via document.documentElement.classList) swaps every CSS variable defined under the .dark selector. Primitives keep reading var(--background), var(--foreground), and so on — they don't know which mode they're in. No JavaScript re-render, no per-component theme prop.
howItWorks.p2: Three modes are supported. Light: no class on <html>. Dark: .dark class on <html>. System: matches the user's prefers-color-scheme media query and re-evaluates whenever the OS preference changes.

liveDemo.title: Live demo
liveDemo.description: Click the toggle and watch the preview card update. Your choice persists across page reloads.
liveDemo.hint: Light, Dark, or System — System follows your OS preference and updates if you change it.

preview.title: Preview card
preview.body: Background, foreground, border, and primary tokens all swap when you toggle. The Card, Button, and Badge primitives stay identical — only the tokens change.

wiring.title: Wiring the toggle
wiring.intro: Drop in <ThemeToggle /> anywhere — usually in the app header or sidebar footer. The component composes our DropdownMenu and Button primitives; no extra setup needed beyond the ThemeProvider already mounted in main.tsx.
wiring.custom: For a custom UI (a switch, a segmented control, a setting page), reach for the useTheme hook directly. It exposes the stored choice and a setter — that's the entire surface area.

api.title: useTheme() returns
api.setTheme: Persists the new choice to localStorage and updates the <html> class.
api.note: useTheme does not return a resolved theme; when "system" is active, derive the resolved mode by reading window.matchMedia("(prefers-color-scheme: dark)") yourself if you need to.

persistence.title: Persistence

storage.before: The selected mode is stored in localStorage under the key
storage.after: . Reloads, new tabs, and same-origin navigation all pick up the stored value.

persistence.coldLoad: On cold load, the stored value is read inside the ThemeProvider's initial useState — so the correct class is on <html> before the first React render commits. The trade-off: if you read this value from JS only (not from an inline <script> in index.html), a brief flash of the wrong theme is possible. See Caveats below.
persistence.system: When "system" is selected, the resolved theme tracks prefers-color-scheme live. Toggling your OS appearance updates the app without a reload.

body.before: Every semantic CSS variable (--background, --foreground, --primary, --border, --ring, etc.) has both a :root and .dark definition. See the full token grid on the

flash.title: Flash of incorrect theme
flash.body: If you skip the early inline script, dark-mode users see a brief light flash on first paint. The minimal pre-paint script below sets the .dark class before <body> renders, matching what ThemeProvider would do on mount.

body.before: A brand override class (.brand-dpds, .brand-tenant-x, etc.) composes with .dark via CSS specificity — no class-order rules to remember. See

theming.title: Theming
theming.description: How the design system stays consistent in light, dark, and brand-customised modes.

architecture.title: Token architecture
architecture.p1: CSS custom properties (variables) are the single source of truth for visual style. Every primitive in the library reads from these variables — no hardcoded colours, no inline hex codes. If a button looks wrong, the fix is in the tokens, not the component.
architecture.p2: Switching between light and dark, or between a default brand and a customer brand, is just a class on the document root that swaps the variable values. Components never know which mode they're in; they only know which tokens to read.

layers.title: Token layers
layers.intro: Tokens live in two layers. Compose primitives against the semantic layer; reach into the palette only when defining a new semantic token.

palette.title: Palette tokens
palette.body: Raw colour values for the full ramp (--color-primary-50 through --color-primary-900, --color-slate-50 through --color-slate-900, etc.). These are stable identifiers for colours, not for purposes.

semantic.title: Semantic tokens
semantic.body: Purpose-based aliases that point at palette values (--background, --primary, --border, --ring, etc.). Each one describes a role in the UI — not a colour name.

rule.title: Override the semantic layer, not the palette.
rule.body: A brand override should swap --primary, --primary-foreground, --ring — not --color-primary-500. Editing the palette breaks the ramp invariants every other consumer depends on.

list.intro: The semantic tokens actually present in src/index.css today are listed below. Extra tokens used for charts, the sidebar, and focus rings exist in the same file; the table in the next section covers the core ones used by most primitives.

modes.title: Light + Dark token tables
modes.description: Six representative tokens side-by-side in both modes — enough to see the swap mechanism at a glance. The full semantic catalogue (background, foreground, card, popover, primary, secondary, muted, accent, destructive, warning, border, input, ring, plus the sidebar set) lives on the Colors foundation.
modes.fullCatalogue: See the full token catalogue on Colors

tailwind.title: Tailwind mapping
tailwind.description: Semantic tokens are exposed as Tailwind utility classes. Use the class form in JSX; reach for var(--token) only when you need it inline.

override.title: Overriding tokens
override.description: Drop this CSS into your app's stylesheet (after the design system's CSS) to swap brand colors. The :root block replaces the system's defaults; the .dark block keeps dark mode in sync.

multiTenant.title: Multi-tenant theming
multiTenant.intro: When multiple brands coexist on the same document, scope overrides to a class instead of :root. Brand classes compose with dark mode via CSS specificity — class order in your HTML doesn't matter.
multiTenant.scope: Apply the brand class at <html> for whole-app theming, or scope it to a subtree when an embedded surface (a customer-branded portal living inside the internal app) needs its own theme without leaking to siblings.

rules.title: Usage rules
rules.description: When to use which token, and what to avoid.

items.semantic: Prefer semantic tokens (bg-primary, text-foreground) over palette tokens (bg-primary-500). Semantic tokens swap on dark mode and brand override; palette tokens don't.
items.contrast: Pair foreground with its matching background — text-foreground on bg-background, text-primary-foreground on bg-primary. These pairs are tested for AA contrast; arbitrary combinations are not.
items.brand: Customizing brand color is a one-line override on --primary in your app stylesheet. Don't fork the component library to change colors.
items.status: destructive, warning, and the status palettes (error, success, informative) carry meaning — don't use them for decorative accents. A red border that isn't an error confuses users.
items.darkparity: Every semantic token has both a :root and a .dark value. When adding a new semantic token, write both at the same time — never ship a light-only token and 'fix dark mode later'.

projectStructure.title: Project Structure
projectStructure.description: How this design system is organised, and the layout we recommend for apps that consume it.

repo.title: This repo's layout
repo.intro: If you're contributing back to the design system, here's how this repo is organised. Folders sit under src/; each one has a single responsibility.

componentsUi.title: components/ui/
componentsUi.body: Every primitive component lives here — Button, Input, Card, Dialog, Tag, and so on. These are the leaf components the rest of the site composes from. Nothing app-specific belongs here; if a component knows about tickets or customers, it goes somewhere else.

componentsDocs.title: components/docs/
componentsDocs.body: Helpers that only the doc site uses — ComponentPage, FoundationPage, DocsPage, PatternPage, Section, CodeBlock, PreviewBlock, PropsTable, RelatedLinks, UsesTokens. Consumers of the library never import from here.

pages.title: pages/
pages.body: One file per route in the doc site. Pages are thin: they compose docs helpers, primitives, and i18n strings. No business logic, no data fetching.

patterns.title: patterns/
patterns.body: Full-screen pattern compositions (login, signup, dashboards). Each pattern is a self-contained component that consumers can drop in. Patterns import from components/ui/, never the reverse.

locales.title: locales/
locales.body: i18n strings — en.json today, ar.json for RTL. Every visible string on every page resolves through one of these files; no hardcoded English in JSX.

data.title: data/
data.body: Static JSON or TypeScript modules — changelog entries, sample dashboard data. Treat this as a stand-in for whatever real data source your app will use.

lib.title: lib/
lib.body: Tiny utilities shared across the codebase. cn() lives here. If something grows beyond a few helpers, promote it to its own folder.

hooks.title: hooks/
hooks.body: Shared React hooks — useIsRtl, theme helpers, anything reused by more than one component. App-specific hooks belong in your app's folder, not here.

consumer.title: Recommended layout for consuming apps
consumer.intro: If you're building a Dubai Police app that uses this library, here's a folder layout that scales well as the app grows.

wrapNotFork.title: Wrap, don't fork
wrapNotFork.p1: Extend design-system components by composition in features/, never by editing source in ui/. If you need a CustomerTable with bespoke columns, build it in features/ and compose the DS Table primitive — don't copy table.tsx into your app and add columns there.
wrapNotFork.p2: When a DS component evolves (new variant, fixed bug, accessibility improvement), forks miss the update. Compositions in features/ pick up DS changes automatically because they import from ui/.
wrapNotFork.p3: If you find yourself unable to compose something — a primitive is missing a prop, a variant isn't expressive enough — open a PR against the design system. That's how every team benefits, instead of one app shipping a bespoke fix nobody else can use.

input.title: Input
input.description: Single-line text entry. The workhorse field for ticket references, search bars, customer names, and short answers.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A default text input.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a CRM customer-search input.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare text input with a placeholder.

withLabel.label: With label
withLabel.description: Always pair an Input with a Label for accessibility — link them via htmlFor + id.

withHelper.label: With helper text
withHelper.description: A short description below the field. Use for format hints (e.g. expected ID format).

withError.label: With error state
withError.description: Set aria-invalid="true" and show the error in red below the field.

password.label: Password with visibility toggle
password.description: Compose with a useState + button overlay. No separate password primitive.

search.label: Search with icon
search.description: Position a leading icon absolutely and pad the input with ps-9.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Greyed out and not focusable. Use when the field is conditionally locked.

readOnly.label: Read-only
readOnly.description: Visible and copyable but not editable. Use for system-generated values.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the Input component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Input is a native <input>. All keyboard, screen-reader, and form behavior comes for free.

items.label: Always pair Input with Label via htmlFor + id. Screen readers and click-to-focus depend on it.
items.error: Mark invalid inputs with aria-invalid="true" and show an error message immediately below the field. Pair with aria-describedby when the error has its own id.
items.autocomplete: Use the autoComplete attribute for known fields (autoComplete="email", "tel", "name") so password managers and form-fillers work.
items.disabled: Disabled inputs are removed from tab order and not announced. If you need users to know why a field is locked, render the explanation in adjacent helper text.
items.placeholder: Placeholders are not labels. They disappear on focus and have low contrast — never rely on them to communicate required information.

select.title: Select
select.description: Pick a single option from a list. Use for filters, status pickers, assignee dropdowns, and any field with a fixed set of choices.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A basic Select with three status options.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: A priority filter — fires onValueChange whenever the user picks an item.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Variants for the patterns you'll see most in a CRM.

default.label: With label
default.description: Always pair with a Label, just like Input.

placeholder.label: Placeholder
placeholder.description: Use a verb + noun ("Filter by status") to clarify what picking does.

grouped.label: Grouped options
grouped.description: Use SelectGroup + SelectLabel + SelectSeparator to organise long lists.

withIcons.label: With icons
withIcons.description: Icons help users scan status or priority lists. Match the icon to the meaning of the value.

controlled.label: Controlled
controlled.description: Pair value + onValueChange when the parent owns the state (e.g. URL query params).

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Locks the trigger. Pair with a tooltip explaining why if the lock is conditional.

props.title: API
props.description: Props on the Select root + key children.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Select is a fully-managed Radix combobox — keyboard, focus trapping, and ARIA are handled.

items.keyboard: Open with Enter/Space/Down; navigate items with Arrow keys; close with Escape or Tab.
items.label: Wire SelectTrigger to a Label via htmlFor + id, exactly like an Input.
items.placeholder: Placeholders in SelectValue are not announced as labels. Use a real Label alongside.
items.searchable: Built-in selects are not searchable. Use Combobox when the list has more than ~15 items.

card.title: Card
card.description: Container for grouped content. Use for ticket previews, customer profiles, dashboard widgets, and stat tiles.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A basic Card with a header and body.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: A minimal ticket card with title + description + content.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common compositions. Mix Header / Title / Description / Action / Content / Footer freely.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Header + title + body content. The minimum viable Card.

headerFooter.label: With header + footer
headerFooter.description: Footer renders with a top border and muted background — good for primary actions or metadata.

withAction.label: With action
withAction.description: CardAction sits top-right in the header. Use for kebab menus, close buttons, or quick actions.

withAvatar.label: With avatar
withAvatar.description: Compose an avatar inside CardTitle. Keep the title content semantic — readers still see the name.

hoverable.label: Interactive
hoverable.description: Add cursor-pointer + hover:shadow-lg. If clicking the whole card navigates, wrap it in a Link.

statTile.label: As a Stat Tile
statTile.description: Pattern for KPI tiles. Description on top, large number as the title. The dedicated Stat / KPI Tile page (linked below) builds on this.

props.title: API
props.description: Props on the Card root. Sub-components accept all native div attributes.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Card itself is decorative. The semantics live in the content inside.

items.heading: If the Card has a CardTitle, render it as a heading element when it acts as a landmark — e.g. an <h3> or <h4> via asChild or by passing the right tag.
items.interactive: If clicking the whole Card navigates somewhere, the entire interactive surface must be a single <a> or <button> (not a div with onClick). Wrap in a Link.
items.contrast: Background is bg-white / dark:bg-zinc-900. Don't override to low-contrast colors that fail body-text WCAG ratios.

tabs.title: Tabs
tabs.description: Switch between sibling views in the same surface — ticket overview / documents / activity, or filter buckets like Open / Closed.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A default horizontal tab set with three panels.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: A ticket detail surface with three tabs.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Variants for in-card vs. page-level navigation.

default.label: Default
default.description: Pill-style tabs with a filled background. Use inside cards or panels.

line.label: Line / underline
line.description: TabsList variant="line" — underline indicator, transparent background. Use for page-level navigation.

vertical.label: Vertical
vertical.description: orientation="vertical" lays TabsList beside TabsContent. Good for settings screens with many tabs.

withIcons.label: With icons
withIcons.description: Icons help scan-readability. Use lucide icons sized size-4.

withBadges.label: With badges
withBadges.description: Show counts (open, overdue) directly in tab labels. Keep them short — they steal space from the label.

controlled.label: Controlled
controlled.description: Pair value + onValueChange when the parent owns the active tab (URL hash, etc.).

props.title: API
props.description: Tabs is built on base-ui — props mirror that library's Root / List / Trigger / Panel API.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Tabs render as semantic role="tablist" + role="tab" elements. Keyboard support is automatic.

items.keyboard: Arrow keys move between tabs; Home/End jump to first/last; Tab moves into the active panel.
items.aria: Tabs and panels are linked automatically — don't add your own aria-controls / aria-labelledby.
items.focus: Focus rings are visible by default. Don't remove them.
items.notNav: Tabs are not navigation. Don't use them for routing — use Sidebar or Breadcrumb.

tooltip.title: Tooltip
tooltip.description: Brief, non-interactive description on hover or focus. Use for icon-only buttons, truncated text, and keyboard-shortcut hints.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Hover the button to see the tooltip.
preview.label: Hover me

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: An icon-only Copy button — the canonical case where a Tooltip is mandatory.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Four common patterns. Wrap multiple Tooltips with a single TooltipProvider to share delay timing.

placement.label: Placement
placement.description: side="top" | "right" | "bottom" | "left". The tooltip auto-flips if there isn't room on the chosen side.

iconButton.label: On icon-only buttons
iconButton.description: Mandatory for icon-only buttons — the tooltip IS the label for keyboard users hovering with focus.

rich.label: Rich content
rich.description: TooltipContent accepts any ReactNode. Keep it short — tooltips dismiss on pointer-leave; don't put interactive content inside.

delay.label: Custom delay
delay.description: TooltipProvider delayDuration controls hover-before-open time. Lower (150ms) for icon buttons; default (700ms) for less essential hints.

props.title: API
props.description: Tooltip props come from base-ui / Radix. The most useful knobs are below.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Tooltips appear on hover AND focus. They're announced to screen readers via aria-describedby.

items.touch: Tooltips are pointer-only. They don't appear on touch. Never put essential information in a tooltip.
items.essential: If the content is required to understand the trigger (e.g. an icon-only button), put it in the tooltip AND in an aria-label so touch + screen-reader users get it.
items.label: aria-label on the trigger and the tooltip text should match. Don't duplicate them visually — pick one.
items.interactive: Don't put buttons or links inside a Tooltip. The tooltip dismisses on pointer-leave, making the inside unreachable. Use Popover or Hover Card instead.

toast.title: Toast
toast.description: Brief, non-blocking confirmations and errors. Implemented with Sonner — call toast(), toast.success(), toast.error(), etc. from anywhere.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Click the button to fire a toast.
preview.label: Ticket saved

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Mount the Toaster once in App.tsx, then import { toast } anywhere.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Wire a success toast to a save action.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Each button fires a toast on the bottom-right of the screen. Toggle dark mode to see how the toasts adapt.

default.label: Default
default.description: Neutral message. Use when the operation is informational and doesn't fit success/warning/error.

success.label: Success
success.description: Confirms an operation completed. Pair with a description showing what was affected.

warning.label: Warning
warning.description: Heads-up that's not blocking. SLA breaches, soft validation, etc.

error.label: Error
error.description: An operation failed. Always include a description with the cause and a recovery hint.

info.label: Info
info.description: Side-channel updates — new comments, status changes from other users.

withAction.label: With action
withAction.description: Pair with an action button (Undo, View). The toast dismisses on click.

promise.label: Promise
promise.description: Loading → success/error driven by a Promise. Sonner manages the state transitions for you.

props.title: API
props.description: Options passed to toast() / toast.success() / etc. as the second argument.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Toasts render in an aria-live region so screen readers announce them.

items.live: Announced via aria-live="polite" by default. Use { duration: Infinity } sparingly — it bypasses auto-dismiss but doesn't change the live-region behaviour.
items.dismiss: Toasts are dismissable by pointer (X button) and keyboard (Escape when focused). Don't rely on auto-dismiss for critical information.
items.essential: Never put critical information (errors that block work) in a toast alone. Pair with an inline Alert or Banner that persists.
items.duration: Default 4s. Longer messages need more time — use { duration: 8000 } when the description is more than one sentence.

dialog.title: Dialog
dialog.description: Modal surface for focused tasks — confirmations, short forms, and detailed views. Includes the Alert Dialog pattern for destructive confirmations.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Click the button to open the dialog.
preview.label: Open dialog

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: An archive-ticket confirmation. DialogTrigger and DialogClose use the render prop, not asChild.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common dialog patterns including the Alert Dialog variant (destructive confirmation).

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Header + body + a Close button in the footer. The X in the top-right is on by default.

withForm.label: With form
withForm.description: Embed a short form. For long forms use a Drawer / Sheet instead — dialogs are for focused, quick tasks.

destructive.label: Destructive (Alert Dialog)
destructive.description: Required confirmation for irreversible actions. Use red icon + destructive button + explicit "Yes, delete" copy. No X close button so the user must pick.

scrollable.label: Scrollable content
scrollable.description: Wrap long content in a div with overflow-y-auto + max-h-*. Header and footer stay pinned.

size.label: Wide
size.description: Override the default width with max-w-* on DialogContent (max-w-2xl, max-w-3xl, etc.).

props.title: API
props.description: Props on Dialog (Root), DialogTrigger, DialogClose, and DialogContent.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Dialog is built on base-ui — focus trap, ARIA, and Escape-to-close are wired up.

items.focus: Focus moves into the dialog on open and returns to the trigger on close. Don't disable focus management.
items.escape: Escape closes the dialog by default. For required-decision flows (destructive confirmations), set modal=true and remove the Close button to force a choice.
items.title: Always provide a DialogTitle. Screen readers announce it when the dialog opens.
items.destructive: Use Alert-Dialog styling (destructive button + warning icon + explicit copy) for irreversible actions. The default Dialog is too permissive for delete/archive flows.
items.modal: modal=true (default) makes the background inert. Only set modal=false for non-blocking surfaces (e.g. floating help panels).

sidebar.title: Sidebar
sidebar.description: App-level vertical navigation rail. Collapsible to icons, responsive (slides into a Sheet on mobile), and persists state via cookie.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A scaled-down sample. The real Sidebar uses fixed positioning + the SidebarProvider context — see the left rail of this page for the live component.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Wrap your app once at the layout level. SidebarProvider must be a common ancestor of Sidebar and your main content.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Visual mocks showing each variant. Code snippets use the real Sidebar API.

collapsible.label: Collapsible to icons
collapsible.description: collapsible="icon" shrinks the sidebar to an icon rail on desktop. Use SidebarTrigger to toggle.

sectionsDividers.label: Sections + dividers
sectionsDividers.description: Group items by purpose. SidebarSeparator between SidebarGroups, SidebarGroupLabel for the section header.

nested.label: Nested items
nested.description: Use SidebarMenuSub for second-level navigation. Limit depth to 2 — past that, switch to a hierarchical Tree view.

badges.label: Badges + footer
badges.description: SidebarMenuBadge for inline counts (open tickets, unread, etc.). Render account/user info in SidebarFooter.

activeState.label: Active state
activeState.description: Pass isActive based on react-router's useLocation. Pair SidebarMenuButton with asChild + NavLink for the cleanest setup.

props.title: API
props.description: The most useful props across the Sidebar family.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Sidebar uses a semantic <nav> landmark + keyboard shortcut + responsive Sheet on mobile.

items.shortcut: Cmd/Ctrl+B toggles the sidebar by default. Document this in your help / onboarding.
items.landmark: The Sidebar renders inside a <nav> landmark. Don't wrap it in another nav.
items.tooltip: When collapsed to icons, SidebarMenuButton.tooltip becomes the only label visible. Provide it for every menu item.
items.current: Use isActive to mark the current route. The component sets aria-current="page" automatically.
items.mobile: On mobile the Sidebar renders inside a Sheet. Focus traps and Escape-to-close are wired up automatically.

form.title: Form
form.description: There's no <Form> wrapper — the project pattern is react-hook-form + zod + Field helpers composed manually. This page documents that pattern.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A live two-field form with Zod validation. Submitting clears the form on success.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: A new-ticket form with Zod schema, FieldGroup layout, and per-field error display.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Four common patterns. Each example is a live, working form.

simple.label: Simple
simple.description: Two fields + submit. The smallest viable form using Field + register + handleSubmit.

validation.label: With validation errors
validation.description: Zod schema with regex. mode="onBlur" so errors appear after the user leaves the field, not on every keystroke.

asyncSubmit.label: Async submit
asyncSubmit.description: isSubmitting from formState drives the disabled + spinner. Don't roll your own loading state.

conditional.label: Conditional fields
conditional.description: watch() a field, then conditionally render dependent fields. Useful for priority-driven escalation reasons.

props.title: API
props.description: Key APIs from react-hook-form + the Field helper family.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: react-hook-form + Field cover the essentials, but each field still needs a real label and an aria-invalid wire-up.

items.label: Every input gets a FieldLabel with htmlFor matching the input id. Screen readers + click-to-focus depend on it.
items.invalid: Pass aria-invalid={!!errors.fieldName} on the input. Field's error state styling keys off it.
items.error: FieldError accepts the errors array directly. Don't roll your own error markup — accessibility roles + aria-describedby would diverge.
items.required: If a field is required by the schema, mark it visually (asterisk on the Label) AND with required on the input. Screen readers need both.
items.submit: Disable the submit button while isSubmitting is true. Re-enable on both success and error so users can retry.

table.title: Table
table.description: Basic semantic table primitives — Table, TableHeader, TableBody, TableRow, TableHead, TableCell. For sortable, filterable, paginated tables use the Data Table on /ui/data-table (coming soon).

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Three CRM tickets in a basic table.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Primitives are exported from data-table.tsx (a future cleanup may move them into their own file).

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Map an array to TableRow + TableCell. No state, no sorting — just semantic HTML with styling.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Six patterns covering most CRM tables.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Header + body. Use font-mono on ID columns to align reference numbers.

striped.label: Striped rows
striped.description: Add a bg-muted/30 className to every other row. Improves scannability for long lists.

selection.label: Row selection
selection.description: Add a checkbox column. Set data-state="selected" on selected rows for the highlight style.

stickyHeader.label: Sticky header
stickyHeader.description: Wrap the Table in an overflow-y-auto container and add `sticky top-0 bg-background z-10` to TableHeader.

empty.label: Empty state
empty.description: A single row with colSpan covering all columns + an Inbox icon + helper copy.

rowActions.label: Row actions
rowActions.description: Last column right-aligned with a kebab Button that opens a DropdownMenu.

props.title: API
props.description: All Table parts accept their native HTML attributes plus className.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: These are semantic table elements — screen readers and keyboard navigation come from the browser.

items.semantic: Use Table for tabular data only. Don't use a Table for layout — use CSS grid/flex.
items.caption: For complex tables, add a <caption> as the first child of <table>. It describes the table to screen readers.
items.checkbox: Each row checkbox needs an aria-label describing what's being selected (e.g. "Select REQ-2025-0142").
items.iconActions: Kebab / actions buttons need an aria-label. "Actions for REQ-2025-0142" — never just "Actions".
items.responsive: Horizontal scroll is automatic via the wrapper div. On narrow screens, consider switching to a List on mobile and Table on desktop.

button.title: Button
button.description: Trigger an action or event with a single click. Buttons support multiple hierarchies, sizes, icons, and the asChild slot pattern.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A live button rendered with the default variant and size.
preview.label: Click me

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Button primitive from the design system.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Minimum viable snippet — render a Button with a label and an onClick handler.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: The primary call-to-action — uses the filled variant.

secondary.label: Secondary
secondary.description: Lower-emphasis action — uses the gray variant.

outline.label: Outline
outline.description: Neutral outline for secondary actions on busy backgrounds.

ghost.label: Ghost
ghost.description: Minimal background — pair with icons or use inside dense toolbars.

destructive.label: Destructive
destructive.description: Irreversible or dangerous actions — uses the filledDestructive variant.

withIcon.label: With icon
withIcon.description: Pair a label with a leading or trailing icon. Use size-4 icons inside default-sized buttons.

loading.label: Loading
loading.description: Compose a loading state with a spinner and disabled attribute while async work runs.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Visually inert and not focusable. Browser-native disabled — no extra ARIA needed.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the Button component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Button is a native <button> element by default and inherits all native semantics.

items.keyboard: Activated by Enter or Space when focused.
items.focus: Focus ring is visible by default (focus-visible:ring-2). Don't remove the ring without an equivalent indicator.
items.aria: The button role is implicit; do not add role="button". Use the native disabled attribute rather than aria-disabled when the action should not run.
items.nesting: Don't nest interactive elements inside a Button (no <a> or <button> children). Use the asChild prop with a single interactive child if you need to render as a link.
items.label: Icon-only buttons must include an aria-label describing the action.

textarea.title: Textarea
textarea.description: Multi-line text entry. Use for comments, notes, descriptions, and any free-form content longer than a single line.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A default textarea that grows with content.
preview.placeholder: Add an internal note. Customers won't see this.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an internal note on a ticket.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare textarea with a placeholder. Auto-grows as the user types.

withLabel.label: With label
withLabel.description: Always pair a Textarea with a Label for accessibility — link them via htmlFor + id.

withHelper.label: With helper text
withHelper.description: Short guidance below the field. Use for format constraints or examples.

withError.label: With error state
withError.description: Set aria-invalid="true" and show the error in red below the field.

autoResize.label: Auto-resize
autoResize.description: The base Textarea uses CSS field-sizing-content, so it grows with content automatically — no JS needed.

withCounter.label: With character counter
withCounter.description: Pair with a controlled value to show a live character count. Useful when maxLength is set.

fixedRows.label: Fixed rows
fixedRows.description: Pass a rows prop to lock the visible height instead of letting it auto-grow.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Greyed out and not focusable. Use when the field is conditionally locked.

readOnly.label: Read-only
readOnly.description: Visible and copyable but not editable. Use for system-generated content.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the Textarea component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Textarea is a native <textarea>. All keyboard, screen-reader, and form behavior comes for free.

items.label: Always pair Textarea with Label via htmlFor + id. Screen readers and click-to-focus depend on it.
items.error: Mark invalid fields with aria-invalid="true" and show the error message immediately below. Use aria-describedby to tie the error to the field when the message has its own id.
items.counter: When showing a character counter, expose it to screen readers via aria-live="polite" if the count is critical (e.g. nearing the limit). Static counters can be plain text.
items.disabled: Disabled textareas are removed from tab order and not announced. If users need to know why a field is locked, render the explanation in adjacent helper text.
items.placeholder: Placeholders disappear on focus and have low contrast — never rely on them to communicate required information. Use them for examples, not labels.

checkbox.title: Checkbox
checkbox.description: A toggle for opt-in choices, multi-select filters, and "select all" patterns. Built on Radix Checkbox.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Checkbox paired with a Label — the standard pattern.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a marketing-consent checkbox with helper text.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare checkbox with no label. Almost always wrap with a Label in real UI.

withLabel.label: With label
withLabel.description: Link Checkbox and Label via id + htmlFor so clicking the text toggles the box.

withDescription.label: With description
withDescription.description: Label plus secondary helper text. Use for consent fields and settings toggles.

indeterminate.label: Indeterminate
indeterminate.description: Pass checked="indeterminate" for the partially-checked visual. The next click resolves to true.

selectAll.label: Select all
selectAll.description: Parent reflects the state of its children: all → checked, some → indeterminate, none → unchecked.

group.label: Group / multi-select filter
group.description: Multiple independent checkboxes — the classic filter-facet pattern.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Disabled checkboxes are removed from tab order. Mirror the dimmed look on the linked Label.

required.label: Required
required.description: Set required to participate in native HTML form validation.

props.title: API
props.description: Props are forwarded to the Radix Checkbox root. The most useful ones:

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Checkbox renders the appropriate ARIA roles and states via Radix. The hard parts are labeling and grouping — handle them in your markup.

items.label: Always pair Checkbox with Label via htmlFor + id. Without it, click target is just the small box and screen readers have no name to announce.
items.indeterminate: Indeterminate is purely visual — the user cannot type into it. Use it to represent a derived state (e.g. parent of partially-selected children), not as a third user-selectable state.
items.keyboard: Native keyboard behavior: Tab to focus, Space to toggle. Enter does not toggle a checkbox by design — don't try to make it.
items.disabled: Disabled checkboxes are removed from tab order and not announced. If users need to know why a field is locked, render the reason in adjacent helper text.
items.group: For multi-select groups, wrap related checkboxes in a fieldset with a legend (or use role="group" with aria-labelledby) so screen readers can announce the group name.

radio.title: Radio
radio.description: A single-choice selector for short, mutually exclusive option sets. Built on Radix RadioGroup. Reach for Select when there are more than ~6 options.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A priority selector with a sensible default.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import both the group root and item primitives.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a ticket priority selector.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare group with no preselected option. The user must pick one before any state exists.

defaultValue.label: With default value
defaultValue.description: Preselect a sensible option via defaultValue so the form has a starting state.

controlled.label: Controlled
controlled.description: Drive the selection from React state via value + onValueChange.

withDescriptions.label: With descriptions
withDescriptions.description: Each option has a label plus helper text. Common for paid/free, fast/cheap tradeoffs in service portals.

horizontal.label: Horizontal layout
horizontal.description: Override the group's display to flex-row for compact yes/no/maybe answers.

disabled.label: Disabled group
disabled.description: Disable the whole group when the field is locked by upstream state.

disabledItem.label: Disabled single item
disabledItem.description: Disable a single option (e.g. unavailable status, no permission) while keeping the rest live.

required.label: Required
required.description: Set required to enforce a selection on native form submit.

props.title: API
props.description: Props split between the group root and individual items.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: RadioGroup renders role="radiogroup" and manages arrow-key navigation between items. The hard parts are labeling and grouping — handle them in your markup.

items.label: Always pair RadioGroupItem with Label via htmlFor + id. Without it the click target is only the small circle and screen readers have no name to announce.
items.keyboard: Tab moves to the group, then arrow keys cycle between items. Space and Enter select the focused item. Don't override these defaults.
items.group: Wrap the RadioGroup in a fieldset with a legend, or set aria-labelledby on the group, so screen readers announce what the group represents.
items.disabled: Disabled items are removed from arrow-key navigation and not announced. If users need to know why an option is unavailable, render the reason in adjacent helper text.
items.checkbox: Use Radio when only one option can be selected at a time. If multiple selections are valid, use Checkbox instead — they aren't interchangeable.

datepicker.title: Datepicker
datepicker.description: A date selector built by composing the Calendar primitive with a Popover trigger. Supports single dates, multi-select, and date ranges via react-day-picker under the hood.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: The standard single-date pattern — Popover trigger + Calendar inside.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Calendar and Popover primitives. Datepicker is a composed pattern, not a single component.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a visit-date picker with a labeled trigger.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

single.label: Single date
single.description: The default pattern. State is a single Date | undefined.

range.label: Date range
range.description: mode="range" returns { from, to }. Show two months side-by-side with numberOfMonths={2}.

multiple.label: Multiple dates
multiple.description: mode="multiple" returns Date[]. Use for picking irregular sets of dates.

withLimits.label: With min/max limits
withLimits.description: Pass a disabled predicate to block past dates, weekends, anything > 90 days out — whatever the rules are.

inline.label: Inline (no popover)
inline.description: Render the Calendar directly. Best for booking screens and dashboards where the picker is always visible.

defaultMonth.label: Default month
defaultMonth.description: Open on a specific month even when no date is selected. Useful when the relevant timeframe is far from today.

dropdownNav.label: Year/month dropdowns
dropdownNav.description: captionLayout="dropdown" turns the header into selects so users can jump to distant dates without paging.

withLabel.label: With label
withLabel.description: Wrap the trigger in a Label + Popover combo for proper field-style markup.

props.title: API
props.description: Calendar forwards all react-day-picker props. The most useful ones for everyday datepicker work:

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: react-day-picker ships keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA labels out of the box. The composition around it is where most a11y gaps appear.

items.label: Pair the trigger with a Label via htmlFor + id so screen readers announce what the date is for.
items.keyboard: Arrow keys move day-by-day, PageUp/PageDown change month, Shift+PageUp/PageDown change year. Don't intercept these.
items.format: Display the selected date in a human-readable format (e.g. "Jun 1, 2026" via date-fns format(d, "PPP")). Raw ISO strings are user-hostile.
items.disabled: Disabled dates are skipped during keyboard navigation. If users need to know why dates are unavailable, render the rule in adjacent helper text.
items.locale: Pass a date-fns locale to internationalize captions and weekday headers — placeholder strings ("Pick a date") still need your own i18n.

inputOtp.title: Input OTP
inputOtp.description: A slot-based input for one-time passcodes, SMS verification, and 2FA. Built on the input-otp package — supports paste, native autocomplete, and mobile keyboards.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A standard 6-digit OTP input.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the four primitives. The container, group, slot, and optional separator all compose together.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an SMS verification field with a digits-only pattern and a 3-3 separator.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: A 6-slot group with no separator and no character restriction.

withSeparator.label: With separator
withSeparator.description: Split into two 3-slot groups with an InputOTPSeparator between them — the standard 6-digit verification look.

digitsOnly.label: Digits only
digitsOnly.description: Restrict input with the REGEXP_ONLY_DIGITS pattern from the input-otp package. The only sensible choice for SMS codes.

fourDigit.label: 4-digit short code
fourDigit.description: Adjust maxLength and slot count for shorter PINs.

controlled.label: Controlled
controlled.description: Drive the value from React state via value + onChange — needed when validating live or syncing to a form library.

onComplete.label: Auto-submit on complete
onComplete.description: onComplete fires once when the last slot fills. Use it to verify the code without making the user press a button.

error.label: Error state
error.description: Set aria-invalid on the slots and surface the error message below the field.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Disables every slot and dims the container.

props.title: API
props.description: Props are forwarded to the underlying input-otp OTPInput. The most useful ones:

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: The input-otp package handles slot navigation, paste-to-fill, and the iOS/Android SMS autofill integration. Your job is labeling, validation, and explanatory text.

items.label: Pair the OTP field with a Label via htmlFor + id so screen readers announce what code the user is entering (e.g. "SMS verification code").
items.autocomplete: Set autoComplete="one-time-code" on the field so iOS and Android can offer to autofill the code from a received SMS.
items.paste: Paste fills every slot in one operation. Don't intercept paste events or apply per-slot validation — the package handles split, trim, and pattern enforcement.
items.error: On a failed verification, set aria-invalid on the slots and render an error message immediately below. Don't clear the field automatically — let the user correct typos.
items.inputMode: Keep inputMode="numeric" for digit-only codes. It gives mobile users the number pad and saves them from hunting through the alphabetical keyboard.

fileUpload.title: File Upload
fileUpload.description: Two primitives for collecting files — FileInput for a single file (passport scan, signed PDF) and MultiFileInput for batches with drag-and-drop (supporting documents, photos). Both keep their own state and surface file name, size, and a remove control.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Both primitives side by side.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Both primitives live in the input module — one import covers them.

singleUsage.title: Usage — single file
singleUsage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a passport scan upload restricted to PDF and images.

multiUsage.title: Usage — multiple files
multiUsage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — supporting documents capped at 5 files.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare input with no accept filter and no label.

withAccept.label: With accept filter
withAccept.description: Restrict the picker to specific extensions or MIME types.

withLabel.label: With label
withLabel.description: Standard form pattern — Label + FileInput linked via htmlFor + id.

withOnChange.label: With onChange handler
withOnChange.description: Read the picked File via onChange. Receives null when the user removes the file.

imagesOnly.label: Images only
imagesOnly.description: Accept any image MIME type via image/* — gives the user the system photo picker on mobile.

default.label: Default
default.description: No limit, no filter, drag-and-drop enabled.

withMaxFiles.label: With maxFiles cap
withMaxFiles.description: Set a hard ceiling. Files beyond the cap are silently ignored — surface a message in your own UI if needed.

withAccept.label: With accept filter
withAccept.description: Restrict picker and drop targets to specific extensions.

withOnChange.label: With onChange handler
withOnChange.description: Receive the full file array on every add or remove. Drive form state or upload progress from here.

withLabel.label: With label
withLabel.description: Pair with a Label for accessibility and a clear field purpose.

props.title: API
props.description: Both components are uncontrolled — they keep their own selection state and notify via onChange.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: These wrap native <input type="file"> elements, so the OS picker handles most accessibility automatically. The drop zone is the part you need to think about.

items.label: Pair each input with a Label via htmlFor + id so screen readers announce what kind of file is expected ("Passport scan", "Supporting documents").
items.accept: Be specific with accept — ".pdf,.jpg,.png" is friendlier than image/* because users immediately see what's allowed in the picker.
items.size: Validate file size in onChange and show errors below the field. The components don't enforce any size limit on their own.
items.dragdrop: Drag-and-drop is a visual nicety, not a replacement for the picker. The "Choose a file" button is always available for keyboard and screen-reader users.
items.feedback: After a successful upload, surface progress and confirmation outside the input (toast, status text). Don't rely on the file chip alone to tell users the upload completed.

tag.title: Tag
tag.description: Interactive labels for filtering, segmenting, and tagging records. Unlike Badge (display-only), Tag supports click and removable states — making it the right pick for filter chips and editable label lists.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A tag with a leading icon and the primary color treatment.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a removable tag list on a ticket header.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Neutral display tag with no color emphasis.

withIcon.label: With icon
withIcon.description: Leading Lucide icon. Sized at 14px regardless of tag size.

selected.label: Selected
selected.description: Stronger background + border for active multi-select filter state.

removable.label: Removable
removable.description: X button on the right calls onRemove. Try removing all three — they regenerate when you refresh.

variants.label: Color variants
variants.description: Six color treatments. Default for neutral labels, status colors carry meaning (don't decorate with destructive).

sizes.label: Sizes
sizes.description: sm (24px) for dense rows and table cells; md (28px) for card headers and filter bars.

group.label: Tag group (multi-select)
group.description: Live demo — click priorities to toggle selection. Mirrors the typical filter-chip pattern.

ticketTags.label: Ticket tags
ticketTags.description: Status-colored tags with icons — the canonical CRM use case.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the Tag component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Tag renders as <button> when clickable, <span> otherwise. Most a11y considerations come from how you compose it — labels and color meaning are your responsibility.

items.button: When you pass onClick, the Tag becomes a real <button> element with keyboard focus and Enter/Space activation. No additional wiring needed.
items.removeLabel: The X (remove) button has aria-label="Remove" by default. If your tag's content is non-obvious from context ("Tag 5" rather than "VIP"), wrap the group in a visible heading so screen-reader users know what's being removed from where.
items.colorMeaning: Color is not the only signal. A destructive Tag should also have a clear text label ("SLA Breach", not just a red color) so colorblind users get the meaning.
items.keyboard: Interactive tags receive Tab focus, activate with Enter or Space. The X button receives its own tab stop with the same activation keys.

banner.title: Banner
banner.description: Full-width, page-level notice for system-wide context — scheduled downtime, trial expiry, sync state. Distinct from Alert (inline within a page section) and Toast (transient, floating).

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A scheduled-maintenance banner with an action and dismiss button.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a trial-ending warning with upgrade CTA.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

info.label: Info
info.description: Neutral system announcements and feature heads-ups.

success.label: Success
success.description: Confirmation of a recently completed bulk operation.

warning.label: Warning
warning.description: Pre-action notice — something needs the user's attention soon.

error.label: Error
error.description: Blocking issue — connectivity, sync failure, expired session.

withAction.label: With action
withAction.description: Trailing Button stays inline on desktop, stacks below the description on mobile.

dismissible.label: Dismissible
dismissible.description: Live demo — close it, then restore. Persisting dismissal across reloads is your app's job.

descriptionOnly.label: Description only
descriptionOnly.description: Skip the title for short, single-line notices.

persistent.label: Persistent error
persistent.description: Blocking errors should not be dismissible — the issue isn't resolved by hiding the banner.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the Banner component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Banner ships with role="status" so assistive tech treats it as a status region. You're responsible for matching the tone to the message — and not abusing it.

items.role: role="status" announces banner content politely. For blocking errors that need immediate user response, wrap a Dialog around the banner content instead — role="alert" inside a banner is too noisy when multiple banners stack.
items.live: Banners are static once rendered. If the message changes after mount, wrap the description in an aria-live="polite" region so screen readers re-announce.
items.dismiss: The dismiss button has aria-label="Dismiss" by default. Don't dismiss errors automatically — let the user acknowledge.
items.colorMeaning: Color is one of two signals. Pair every variant with a clear text label ("Connection lost", not just a red banner with no text) so colorblind users get the meaning.

emptyState.title: Empty State
emptyState.description: A composable placeholder for surfaces with no content — filtered lists, fresh accounts, permission walls, failed loads. Reach for this whenever the right answer is "there's nothing here yet, and here's what to do next."

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A no-data empty state with a primary action.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — first-visit customers list with an Add CTA.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

noResults.label: No results (after filter)
noResults.description: When filters narrow the list to zero. Always offer a way to clear filters — don't strand the user.

noData.label: No data yet
noData.description: First visit to an empty section. Tell the user what to do next, not just that something's missing.

error.label: Error / failed to load
error.description: Retry + secondary action (report issue, contact support). Keep the message blame-free.

permission.label: Permission denied
permission.description: User can't see the content. Tell them why and what to do — don't just block.

bothActions.label: With primary + secondary action
bothActions.description: Two pathways from the empty state. Primary should be the recommended path; secondary the escape hatch.

customIcon.label: Custom icon
customIcon.description: Override the variant's default with any Lucide glyph or custom illustration.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the EmptyState component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: EmptyState renders a clear heading and centered layout. Most a11y considerations are about content, not structure.

items.heading: The title renders as an <h3>. If your empty state lives inside a section that already has an h2, this nesting is correct. Don't render multiple EmptyState components on the same page — each implies a missing dataset and they'll fight for primary focus.
items.actionLabel: Action button text is the most important copy. "Add customer" beats "Click here". Screen reader users hear it standalone.
items.iconHidden: The icon is decorative (aria-hidden). Screen readers announce the heading and description; the icon is a visual cue only.
items.notHero: This is the compact empty state for inline use (tables, sections, panels). For full-page splash empties — a 404 page, a 'no tickets ever' landing — use EmptyStateHero instead (the larger Lottie-animated variant).

list.title: List
list.description: Composable rows for activity feeds, attachment lists, notifications, and any sequence where Table would be overkill. Pick between dense and comfortable spacing; opt in to dividers and interactivity per use case.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A divided ticket list with avatars, titles, and status badges. Each row is interactive.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the List composition. List.Item, Leading, Content, Title, Description, and Trailing are exposed as dot-notation members.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a ticket list with avatar leading slot, two-line content, and trailing chevron.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

simple.label: Simple (text only)
simple.description: Bare minimum — no leading, no trailing, no descriptions. Useful for compact menus and step lists.

divided.label: With dividers
divided.description: Adds a 1px line between adjacent items. Improves scanability on long lists.

density.label: Dense vs comfortable
density.description: Same content rendered at both densities. Use dense for side rails and tables; comfortable for primary content.

interactive.label: Interactive (hover + keyboard)
interactive.description: interactive={true} adds hover background, cursor, focus ring, role="button", and Enter/Space activation. Pair with onClick.

withAvatars.label: With avatars
withAvatars.description: Avatar in the leading slot — the canonical pattern for comment threads and activity feeds.

withIcons.label: With icons
withIcons.description: Lucide icon in the leading slot — sized at 16px automatically via [&_svg]:size-4.

withBadges.label: With badges / unread dots
withBadges.description: Trailing slot can hold a badge, timestamp, unread indicator, or any combination.

props.title: API
props.description: Props are split between the root and the dot-notation members.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: List uses a native <ul>/<li> structure for screen-reader semantics. Interactivity is opt-in per item — accessibility concerns differ between read-only and clickable lists.

items.semantics: List renders as <ul>; List.Item renders as <li>. Screen readers announce "list, N items" automatically — don't override with role="list".
items.interactive: When interactive is true, items get role="button", tabIndex={0}, and keyboard activation (Enter / Space). They become standalone interactive targets.
items.nestedActions: Place real buttons inside List.Trailing (delete, more menu) — don't make the whole row interactive and nest buttons inside. Nested buttons inside a role="button" parent confuse screen readers and break keyboard semantics.
items.truncation: List.Title and List.Description truncate at one line. If your content needs to wrap, pass a className that removes truncate (e.g. className="whitespace-normal") on the title.

statTile.title: Stat Tile
statTile.description: A KPI tile with a label, value, optional delta indicator, and inline sparkline. Built for dashboard hero rows where multiple metrics need to scan at a glance.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: An open-tickets tile with positive delta, icon, and a 7-point sparkline.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an open-tickets KPI with delta and sparkline.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Just a label and a value. The minimum useful StatTile.

deltaUp.label: Delta up
deltaUp.description: Default tone — up = green = positive. Most metrics work this way.

deltaDown.label: Delta down
deltaDown.description: Default tone — down = red = negative. Use deltaTone when this assumption is wrong.

neutralDelta.label: Neutral delta
neutralDelta.description: Zero change. Renders a right arrow + gray tone — visually distinct from up/down.

toneOverride.label: Tone override (down = good)
toneOverride.description: Response time dropped 18% — that's positive. deltaTone="positive" overrides the direction-based red coloring.

withIcon.label: With icon
withIcon.description: Tinted icon square top-right. Use for tiles that need identity at a glance — score, satisfaction, revenue.

withSparkline.label: With sparkline
withSparkline.description: Inline SVG sparkline at the bottom-right. Stroke uses currentColor — tone via parent text color.

loading.label: Loading
loading.description: Animated skeleton placeholders for label, value, delta, and sparkline. Use while data is fetching.

interactive.label: Interactive (clickable)
interactive.description: Pass onClick to make the whole tile a button with hover + focus state. Use to drill down into a detail view.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the StatTile component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: StatTile renders as a static <div> by default and a real <button> when onClick is set. The most common a11y trap is color meaning — be deliberate about tone overrides.

items.button: When you pass onClick, the tile becomes a focusable <button> with hover + ring state. Screen readers announce the label + value when focused; no extra markup needed.
items.colorMeaning: Color alone shouldn't convey "good" vs "bad". The arrow + delta value carry meaning even without color, but the label should make context clear — "Backlog" reads negative regardless of trend color.
items.sparklineHidden: The sparkline is decorative (aria-hidden). The numeric value and delta tell the story; the line is visual texture. Don't rely on the sparkline for the primary signal.
items.loading: Loading skeletons use opacity animation, not motion. Safe under prefers-reduced-motion. Replace them as soon as data arrives — don't leave them indefinitely.

label.title: Label
label.description: A native <label> wrapper with consistent typography and disabled-state handling. The link between every form control and its accessible name.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Standard pattern — Label + Input linked by htmlFor + id.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a labeled ticket-reference input.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Just text, linked to an input via htmlFor.

required.label: Required
required.description: Add a red asterisk for sighted users plus an sr-only "required" for screen readers. Also set the actual required attribute on the input.

withHelp.label: With helper text
withHelp.description: Pair the label with a short hint on the same baseline — character count, format, or unit.

forCheckbox.label: For a checkbox
forCheckbox.description: Same primitive works for checkboxes and radios. Clicking the label toggles the control.

srOnly.label: Visually hidden (sr-only)
srOnly.description: When a visual label would crowd the layout (a search bar with an icon, a quick-edit cell). The control still gets an accessible name.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: When the linked input is disabled, the label dims automatically via the peer-disabled selector — no extra prop needed.

props.title: API
props.description: Label forwards all native <label> attributes plus className.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Label is a thin wrapper around the native <label> element. The hard parts — click-to-focus, screen-reader announcement, required-state handling — come from getting the markup right, not from the component.

items.htmlFor: Always set htmlFor to the input's id. Without it, the label is decorative — clicking it won't focus the control and screen readers won't announce the pair.
items.required: For required fields, render a visible asterisk (aria-hidden) AND an sr-only "required" text. Don't rely on the asterisk alone — it's invisible to non-visual users.
items.placeholder: Placeholders are not labels. They disappear on focus and have low contrast. Use a real Label even when the placeholder seems descriptive enough.
items.srOnly: When using sr-only, double-check the label still describes what the control does — not just what's visible. A search bar needs a label like "Search tickets", not just "Search".
items.disabled: Disabled inputs are removed from tab order and not announced. If users need to know why a field is locked, render the explanation in adjacent helper text.

switch.title: Switch
switch.description: An on/off toggle for binary settings — notification preferences, feature flags, automation rules. Previously called Toggle; the /forms/toggle route now redirects here.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Switch + Label, the canonical pairing.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an SLA-alerts toggle.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare switch with no label. Almost always wrap with a Label in real UI.

labeled.label: With label
labeled.description: Link Switch and Label via id + htmlFor so clicking the text toggles the switch.

withDescription.label: With description
withDescription.description: Label plus secondary helper text. Use for settings where the consequence isn't obvious from the title alone.

controlled.label: Controlled
controlled.description: Drive the state from React via checked + onCheckedChange.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Both states render in the disabled style. Mirror the dimmed look on the linked Label so the connection is clear.

small.label: Small size
small.description: Use sm in dense settings lists or table rows where the default 24px height is too much.

settingsList.label: In a settings list
settingsList.description: Real-world pattern — four notification preferences as a vertically stacked list with icons, titles, descriptions, and trailing switches.

props.title: API
props.description: Props are forwarded to the Radix Switch root, plus a custom size prop.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Switch renders role="switch" via Radix — screen readers announce "toggle" instead of "checkbox", which matches the semantic difference (a switch acts immediately; a checkbox is a selection in a form).

items.label: Always pair Switch with Label via htmlFor + id. Clicking the label toggles the switch and screen readers announce the pair.
items.keyboard: Tab to focus, Space or Enter to toggle. Default Radix behaviour; don't override.
items.role: Use Switch when the change takes effect immediately (settings, feature flags). Use Checkbox for selections inside a form that submits later — the affordance is different.
items.disabled: Disabled switches are removed from tab order and not announced. Show the reason for the lock in adjacent helper text — "Locked by policy", etc.
items.checkbox: Switch is not a one-of-many selector. For mutually exclusive options use Radio; for multi-select use Checkbox group.

slider.title: Slider
slider.description: A draggable thumb on a track for selecting a number or a numeric range. Use for fuzzy quantities — SLA windows, price ranges, priority weights — where typing an exact value would be slower than dragging.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Single-thumb slider over 0–100.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an SLA-window slider with a live value readout.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare slider with no label. Almost always pair with a Label and a value readout in real UI.

withValueLabel.label: With value label
withValueLabel.description: Controlled value pinned next to the label so the user always sees the current setting.

range.label: Range (two thumbs)
range.description: Pass an array of two numbers as the value — the primitive renders one thumb per array entry.

withSteps.label: With step markers
withSteps.description: Step + a tick row below the track for fixed increments. Useful when the discrete values are meaningful (hours, days, severity).

withMinMax.label: With min / max labels
withMinMax.description: When the scale ends are qualitative (Low / High, Slow / Fast), surface the words below the track.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Dims the track and removes the thumb from tab order. Use when the field is conditionally locked.

vertical.label: Vertical
vertical.description: Orientation vertical needs an explicit height on the className. Layout flips automatically.

props.title: API
props.description: Props are forwarded to the Radix Slider root.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Slider renders role="slider" with proper aria-valuenow / aria-valuemin / aria-valuemax via Radix. The hard parts are labeling and surfacing the current value to non-visual users.

items.label: Pair every slider with a visible Label and a live value readout (the number, not just the position). Screen readers announce the value but sighted users need to see it too.
items.keyboard: Arrow keys move by step; PageUp/PageDown jump by 10 steps; Home/End go to min/max. Don't intercept these.
items.value: Use onValueChange for live updates (display, validation hints) and onValueCommit for expensive side effects (network requests). Firing a request on every drag tick will hammer the API.
items.touch: Default thumb size meets the 24×24px touch-target minimum. If a slider sits in a dense layout, do NOT shrink the thumb below this — add padding instead.
items.alternatives: Sliders are inappropriate for precise values (exact prices, addresses, percentages beyond ~5 ticks). Provide an Input alongside the slider when accuracy matters.

combobox.title: Combobox
combobox.description: A searchable single- or multi-select picker, composed from Popover + cmdk. Reach for it when there are more than ~10 options, or when users need to type to filter.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Assignee picker over a flat list of agents.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Combobox primitives. Built on cmdk + Radix Popover.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an agent assignee picker with single-select + check mark on the selected row.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and states. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Single-select over a flat list. Trigger renders as an outlined Button; clicking it opens the popover.

multiSelect.label: Multi-select
multiSelect.description: Keep your own Set<string> of selected values; toggle entries inside onSelect. The chips below are rendered separately for context.

withGroups.label: With groups
withGroups.description: ComboboxGroup adds an uppercase mono heading above its items. Pair with ComboboxSeparator between groups.

creatable.label: Creatable options
creatable.description: Use the empty state as the create slot — render a 'Create "query"' action when the typed value doesn't match any existing item.

asyncLoading.label: Async loading
asyncLoading.description: Set shouldFilter={false} on ComboboxContent so cmdk's client-side filter doesn't fight your server. Render ComboboxLoading while a request is in flight.

disabled.label: Disabled
disabled.description: Apply disabled to the trigger Button when the field is conditionally locked (e.g. auto-assigned by an SLA rule).

props.title: API
props.description: Combobox is a composition primitive — each piece is a thin wrapper around cmdk or Popover. The most useful props:

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: cmdk handles keyboard navigation, focus management, and ARIA roles for the listbox. The hard parts are labeling the trigger and signalling state to non-visual users.

items.keyboard: Arrow keys move between items; Enter selects; Escape closes. Type to filter — focus stays in the input, items below move with you.
items.search: Always include a ComboboxInput. A combobox without a search affordance is a Select with extra steps — and visually impaired users have no way to find an item in a long list.
items.label: Pair the trigger with a Label or aria-label. Without one, screen readers announce "button" with no context.
items.async: When loading async results, render ComboboxLoading so the live region announces the wait. Don't leave the list empty during the request — it reads as "no results" to screen readers.
items.alternatives: For 2–7 mutually exclusive options, use Radio. For 8–~15, use Select. Reach for Combobox when the list is longer or when typing-to-filter is genuinely faster than scanning.

badge.title: Badge
badge.description: A small inline label for counters, statuses, and tags. Display-only by default — for clickable selectable pills use Tag instead.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Status badges across the common CRM ticket states.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a ticket status badge with a leading dot icon.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

variants.label: Core variants
variants.description: Default, secondary, outline, and ghost. Use these for neutral labels without semantic meaning.

statuses.label: Status variants
statuses.description: Six semantic colors for ticket / case states. Each carries meaning — don't use them for decorative labels.

sizes.label: Sizes
sizes.description: sm for counters inside icon buttons, md for inline status indicators, lg for prominent header labels.

withDot.label: With status dot
