withDot.description: Use a filled circle icon as a leading dot for online/offline-style indicators.

withNumber.label: Counter on icon button
withNumber.description: Position absolutely on a parent button for the classic notification-count pattern.

withIcon.label: With icon
withIcon.description: Lucide icons render at ~12px automatically. Use for status badges where the icon adds meaning beyond the color.

asLink.label: As a link
asLink.description: Use the render prop to render the Badge as an anchor (or any tag) while keeping its styling.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the Badge component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Badge is a presentational <span> by default. The accessibility considerations come from how you use it.

items.semantic: Status colors must match meaning. "success" green for resolved tickets, "danger" red for SLA breaches — swapping these confuses users who learn the color-to-state mapping.
items.label: Color is not enough. Always include text or an icon inside the badge — don't rely on color alone to convey state (WCAG 1.4.1).
items.color: Status text-on-tinted-background hits AA Large contrast at best. For small text and dense layouts, the tinted backgrounds carry the contrast — keep text size at least 12px.
items.interactive: If a Badge is clickable, render it as a button or anchor via the render prop and add a proper aria-label or text content. A clickable <span> with onClick is not keyboard-accessible.
items.tag: Reach for Tag instead when the pill needs onClick / selected / removable. Badge is display-only by design — mixing them is the most common a11y mistake.

avatar.title: Avatar
avatar.description: A circular user representation — image with a graceful fallback to initials. Composes into avatar groups, status indicators, and team marks.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Avatar with image source and initials fallback.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Avatar primitives. Built on Radix Avatar.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an assignee chip with initials fallback.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

withImage.label: With image + fallback
withImage.description: AvatarImage renders the photo; AvatarFallback shows initials while it loads or after a failure.

fallback.label: Initials only
fallback.description: Skip AvatarImage entirely (or use a missing src). Three CRM agents with brand-tinted backgrounds.

sizes.label: Sizes
sizes.description: sm for list items, default for most surfaces, lg for profile cards and detail headers.

withBadge.label: With status dot
withBadge.description: AvatarBadge pins a small dot to the bottom-right corner. Color it via className for status semantics.

statuses.label: Online / away / busy / offline
statuses.description: Compose presence states by tinting AvatarBadge. Pair with adjacent text for screen-reader users — dot color alone is invisible to them.

square.label: Square
square.description: Override rounded-full with rounded-md for a team-mark / org-icon treatment. Both rectangles need matching className on AvatarFallback.

group.label: Avatar group
group.description: Stack avatars with a small overlap; cap with an AvatarGroupCount ("+3") when the team is larger than the visible set.

props.title: API
props.description: Avatar exports six primitives. The most useful props:

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Avatar inherits Radix's accessibility model. The hard parts are the alt text and the presence-dot meaning.

items.alt: Always set a meaningful alt on AvatarImage — the person's full name, not "User avatar". Screen readers and broken-image users both depend on it.
items.fallback: AvatarFallback children must be readable text or an icon — not just CSS. "AH" is fine; an empty background is not.
items.status: Status dots (online / away / busy / offline) carry semantic meaning. Pair with adjacent text or aria-label so colour-blind and screen-reader users get the state.
items.group: AvatarGroupCount renders as a numeric chip. Wrap with role="group" and aria-label ("3 more team members") if the count carries information beyond visual grouping.
items.decoration: Avatars are decorative when they sit next to the user's name. In that case, set alt="" so screen readers don't double-announce.

skeleton.title: Skeleton
skeleton.description: A pulsing rectangle used as a loading placeholder. Compose multiple skeletons to mirror the final layout — text lines, avatar circle, button, card.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Three text-line skeletons of decreasing width — the canonical paragraph placeholder.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a customer-card skeleton with avatar + two text rows.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Building blocks plus full-page loading layouts. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

textLine.label: Text line
textLine.description: The atom of every skeleton layout. Use h-4 for body text, h-3 for captions, h-6+ for headings.

paragraph.label: Paragraph
paragraph.description: Three text lines of decreasing width. Gives the impression of body copy without committing to specific content.

avatar.label: Avatar
avatar.description: Round skeleton via rounded-full. Pair with the size-* utility that matches the real Avatar size.

listItem.label: List item
listItem.description: Avatar + two-line text content + trailing badge. The shape of a typical row in a tickets list.

card.label: Customer card
card.description: Composed loading state — avatar header, paragraph body, two action buttons. Mirrors the production card.

tableRow.label: Table rows
tableRow.description: Five-row table placeholder. Generate the rows with Array.from({ length: 5 }).map(...) so it's easy to tune.

props.title: API
props.description: Skeleton is a styled div — every prop is a standard HTMLDivElement attribute.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: A skeleton is a visual loading hint. Without proper ARIA, screen readers see an empty region and report "loading…" never.

items.live: Wrap the skeleton block in an aria-live="polite" + aria-busy="true" container so screen readers announce the loading state and the eventual content swap.
items.shape: Match the skeleton layout to the final content size and rhythm — a 24px-tall placeholder followed by a 60px chunk of real text causes the page to jump.
items.motion: The pulse animation respects prefers-reduced-motion via Tailwind's animate-pulse — users with that setting see a static block. Don't override.
items.duration: Skeletons should appear only for slow loads (>~200ms). For sub-200ms requests, show nothing — a flash of skeleton is more jarring than no feedback.
items.empty: Skeletons are not empty states. If a request returns no data, use EmptyState — not a permanently-pulsing skeleton.

breadcrumb.title: Breadcrumb
breadcrumb.description: A hierarchical trail showing where the user is in the app. Each step (except the current one) is clickable.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Three-step ticket trail with default "/" separators.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a ticket trail with the current page non-clickable.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Three steps with "/" separators and the current page rendered via BreadcrumbPage.

withHome.label: With home icon
withHome.description: Replace the first step's text with a Home icon. Pair the icon with an sr-only "Home" so screen readers still announce it.

customSeparator.label: Custom separator
customSeparator.description: Pass any node as BreadcrumbSeparator children — a Lucide chevron, a slash glyph, or a vertical bar.

truncated.label: Truncated middle
truncated.description: When the trail is long, collapse the middle steps into a BreadcrumbEllipsis. Keep first + last + current visible.

withDropdown.label: Ellipsis with dropdown
withDropdown.description: Pair the ellipsis with a Dropdown so users can expand the collapsed steps. Best when the middle steps are useful but rarely visited.

currentNonClickable.label: Current page (non-clickable)
currentNonClickable.description: BreadcrumbPage renders a span with aria-current="page" and no href. Don't make the current step a link — it confuses screen-reader users.

props.title: API
props.description: Seven primitives that compose into the trail.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Breadcrumb is a real <nav> landmark with proper ARIA roles. The hard parts are the current-page semantics and icon-only steps.

items.landmark: Breadcrumb renders <nav aria-label="breadcrumb"> — screen readers list it as a navigation landmark. Don't wrap it in another <nav>.
items.current: Always use BreadcrumbPage for the current step. It sets aria-current="page" so assistive tech announces "current page". A regular <a> at the end is invisible to that announcement.
items.separator: Separators are aria-hidden and role="presentation". Don't add them yourself as text inside the link — the primitive handles it.
items.iconOnly: Home and other icon-only links need a sr-only label or aria-label. Otherwise screen readers announce "link" with no destination.
items.truncate: When using BreadcrumbEllipsis, pair it with a Dropdown if the hidden steps matter. A naked ellipsis with no expansion is a dead end for keyboard users.

pagination.title: Pagination
pagination.description: A navigation control for moving through pages of results. Composes numbered links, prev/next buttons, and edge shortcuts.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Numbered pagination over 12 pages with ellipsis truncation in the middle.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — numbered pagination wired to a page state.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

numbered.label: Numbered with ellipsis
numbered.description: The canonical pattern. Shows first, last, and a window of pages around the current one. Click any number to jump.

prevNextOnly.label: Prev / Next only
prevNextOnly.description: Minimal controls plus a textual position. Best for infinite-scroll fallback or when total count is unknown.

firstLast.label: With First / Last
firstLast.description: Add « » shortcuts when users have many pages and skipping to either end is faster than clicking through.

pageSize.label: Page-size selector
pageSize.description: Pair with a Select to let users choose rows per page. Usually lives in the table footer alongside the pagination.

withCaption.label: With X–Y of Z caption
withCaption.description: A short caption above the controls helps users orient — "Showing 21–40 of 247 tickets".

disabledEdges.label: Disabled edges
disabledEdges.description: Set aria-disabled on Previous when at page 0 and Next when at the last page. pointer-events drop automatically via the aria-disabled selector.

props.title: API
props.description: Nine primitives that compose into the control.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Pagination renders a real <nav> landmark. The hard parts are signalling the current page and disabled edges to screen-reader users.

items.landmark: <nav aria-label="pagination"> announces this as a navigation region. Pages can have multiple paginations if they're distinct — give each one its own aria-label.
items.current: Use isActive on the current page — it sets aria-current="page" so screen readers announce "current page, 3" instead of just "3".
items.disabled: aria-disabled is preferred over the disabled attribute on links. It still focuses (so keyboard users can land on the control) but ignores clicks. The primitive handles pointer-events.
items.ellipsis: PaginationEllipsis has aria-hidden + an sr-only "More pages" label so screen readers know there's a gap. Don't render the ellipsis as a clickable target on its own.
items.tabular: Use tabular-nums on the numeric caption ("Showing 21–40 of 247") so digits don't shift width when the numbers change.

stepper.title: Stepper
stepper.description: A horizontal flow indicator for multi-step forms — customer signup, application wizards, checkout. The /ui/progress-tracker route redirects here per Step 2; the vertical Progress Tracker primitive still exists for legacy product pages.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: A 4-step service-portal flow with two completed steps, an active one, and one pending.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Stepper primitive and its StepperStep type.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a trade-license renewal flow.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common states of the flow. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Mid-flow
default.description: Two completed steps, one active (with pulsing ripple), one pending. The connector between completed steps fills with the primary color; between any non-completed pair it's a dashed line.

start.label: Start state
start.description: Only the first step is active; the rest are pending. No completed steps means every connector is dashed.

done.label: All completed
done.description: Every step is completed. Connectors all fill primary; dots all show the check icon.

controlled.label: Controlled flow with Next / Back
controlled.description: Drive the step index from React state; map index → status when building the array.

longFlow.label: Long flow (6 steps)
longFlow.description: Same primitive scales to longer flows. Watch the available width — labels truncate awkwardly above ~7 steps.

props.title: API
props.description: Stepper takes a steps array. Each StepperStep has a title and a status.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Stepper is a visual progress indicator. Pair it with proper headings and form labels — don't rely on the dots alone to communicate where the user is.

items.live: When the step changes, announce "Step X of Y, <title>" via an aria-live region adjacent to the stepper. Screen-reader users don't see the dots advance.
items.titles: Keep step titles short and meaningful. "Customer details" beats "Step 1" — the latter is invisible to assistive tech without context.
items.motion: The active step has a pulsing ripple animation. Framer Motion respects prefers-reduced-motion automatically; users with that setting see a static dot.
items.vertical: Stepper is horizontal-only. For vertical flows with rich step content (descriptions, timestamps, action buttons per step), use the legacy ProgressTracker primitive — documented separately in product pages.
items.color: Dots and connectors carry meaning via color (primary for completed/active, muted for pending). Don't rely on color alone — keep the title label so users can read state without parsing the color.

command.title: Command
command.description: A fast, searchable command palette built on cmdk. Use for global ⌘K shortcuts, action launchers, and quick navigation menus. Combobox is the form-field cousin; Command is the keyboard-driven launcher.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Inline command palette with a single quick-actions group.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Command primitives. Built on cmdk (already a dep from Combobox).

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a global ⌘K palette wired with a keydown listener.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Flat list of actions with a search input above. No groups or shortcuts yet.

groups.label: With groups
groups.description: Two groups separated by a CommandSeparator. Headings render in uppercase mono.

shortcuts.label: With keyboard shortcuts
shortcuts.description: CommandShortcut right-aligns the kbd hint inside each item. Pass the literal shortcut as children.

withIcons.label: With icons
withIcons.description: Leading Lucide icons add visual scanning speed. The primitive sizes svgs to 16px automatically.

empty.label: Empty state
empty.description: CommandEmpty renders when the search doesn't match. Customize the copy for context ("No customers match").

cmdK.label: Global ⌘K palette
cmdK.description: CommandDialog wraps the palette in a Dialog. Wire a keydown listener for cmd/ctrl+K to toggle. Live demo — press the keys with focus on this page.

props.title: API
props.description: Ten primitives that compose into a palette.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: cmdk handles arrow-key navigation, focus management, and ARIA combobox / listbox roles. Pair with proper labels and Dialog semantics when used as a global palette.

items.keyboard: Arrow keys move; Enter selects; Escape closes (when inside a Dialog). Type to filter — focus stays in the input.
items.title: CommandDialog renders a hidden DialogTitle for screen readers ("Command palette" by default). Override the title prop when the palette is scoped (e.g. "Switch customer").
items.shortcuts: Shortcut hints (⌘N, Ctrl+K) are visual cues only. Real keyboard handling is your responsibility — wire keydown listeners that mirror the shortcut.
items.dialog: Inside CommandDialog the palette is modal — focus is trapped, the backdrop blocks the page. Escape closes; clicking outside closes. Standard Radix Dialog semantics.
items.empty: Always include CommandEmpty. Without it, an empty list reads as "loading" to screen readers.

menu.title: Menu
menu.description: A horizontal top-bar navigation menu with optional dropdown content panels. Previously called Navigation Menu — the primitive file is now /components/ui/menu.tsx but exports keep the NavigationMenu* names so existing imports continue to work. For action menus (with icons, shortcuts, destructive items), see DropdownMenu.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Top-bar menu with a Services dropdown plus standalone links.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import from the renamed @/components/ui/menu file. Exports keep their NavigationMenu* names.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — Service Portal top-nav with a Services dropdown and a Tickets standalone link.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common shapes. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

basic.label: Standalone links
basic.description: Three top-level entries with no dropdown content. NavigationMenuLink for direct navigation.

withContent.label: With dropdown content
withContent.description: NavigationMenuTrigger opens a NavigationMenuContent panel. Lay out the panel children yourself — usually a grid of links.

withIcons.label: With icons
withIcons.description: Add leading Lucide icons to links. The primitive sizes svgs to 16px automatically.

active.label: Active item
active.description: Apply data-active to the link for the current route. Pair with your router (react-router-dom NavLink, Next link, etc.) so it stays in sync.

grouped.label: Grouped content
grouped.description: Panels can host multiple grouped sub-sections with their own uppercase headings — common for "Services → Customer-facing / Internal".

props.title: API
props.description: Seven primitives that compose the menu.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Menu uses base-ui's NavigationMenu primitive with built-in keyboard navigation. The hard parts are landmark wrapping and current-page marking.

items.landmark: Wrap Menu in a <nav aria-label="Main"> in your layout so screen readers list it as a navigation landmark. Don't add aria-label to the primitive itself.
items.keyboard: Tab to the menu, arrow keys cycle items, Enter or Space activates. Triggers open on focus + arrow-down; Escape closes. Don't intercept.
items.current: Mark the active route with data-active on NavigationMenuLink. Pair with aria-current="page" on the underlying anchor so assistive tech announces "current page".
items.iconOnly: Icon-only links need an aria-label or sr-only text. Don't ship a link that's only a glyph.
items.dropdown: For action menus (right-click style, with icons + shortcuts + destructive items), use DropdownMenu instead. Menu is for top-bar navigation; DropdownMenu is for action lists.

contextMenu.title: Context Menu
contextMenu.description: A right-click triggered popover for row / cell / area actions. Built on Radix Context Menu — supports nested submenus, checkbox and radio items, destructive actions, and labels.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Right-click the trigger area to open a simple Edit / Duplicate / Delete menu.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Context Menu primitives. Built on Radix Context Menu.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — context-menu actions on a ticket row.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common patterns. Right-click the trigger areas to open each menu.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Three items with a separator and a destructive last action. The minimum viable shape.

withIcons.label: With icons + shortcuts
withIcons.description: Leading Lucide icons plus right-aligned keyboard shortcuts. The primitive sizes svgs to 16px automatically.

submenu.label: Submenu
submenu.description: ContextMenuSub creates a nested popover. SubTrigger renders the chevron automatically; SubContent opens to the side.

checkboxItems.label: Checkbox items
checkboxItems.description: Multi-select within a menu. Controlled via checked + onCheckedChange per item. Useful for view options (pin, mute, archive).

radioItems.label: Radio items
radioItems.description: Mutually exclusive selection. Wrap RadioItems in a RadioGroup with shared value + onValueChange.

destructive.label: Destructive item
destructive.description: Pass destructive on a ContextMenuItem for red tinting. The shortcut still aligns right.

full.label: Full ticket-row menu
full.description: Everything composed — icons + shortcuts + submenu + separator + destructive action. The real-world pattern for table rows.

props.title: API
props.description: Fourteen primitives that compose the menu.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Radix Context Menu ships proper roles, focus management, and keyboard navigation. The hard parts are discoverability and destructive-action confirmation.

items.keyboard: Right-click opens. Inside the menu: arrow keys move; Enter or Space activates; Escape closes; Right-arrow opens a submenu; Left-arrow closes it.
items.discoverable: Context menus are easy to miss. Always provide an alternative path for the same actions (a row hover menu, a dropdown on a button) — don't make right-click the only way to delete or assign.
items.destructive: Destructive items should require confirmation for irreversible operations. Pair Delete with a Dialog confirm, or use a typed-confirm pattern ("type DELETE to confirm").
items.labels: Use ContextMenuLabel to group items with a section heading. Mix-and-match groups (Display / Priority / Actions) within one menu reads better than 12 flat items.
items.touch: Right-click doesn't exist on touch devices. On mobile, expose the same actions via a long-press handler or a per-row trailing menu button.

navbar.title: Navbar
navbar.description: A horizontal application header with brand, nav links, optional search, and a trailing slot for user menu and notifications. New primitive built for the Service Portal — composes with Sidebar, Breadcrumb, and Menu.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Three-slot navbar with brand, nav links, and a user avatar.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a sticky portal header with notifications + user avatar.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common shapes. Each preview shows a full navbar with the marked variation.

basic.label: Brand + links
basic.description: The minimum viable shape. NavbarBrand on the left, NavbarLinks in the middle, a single Avatar on the right.

withSearch.label: With search
withSearch.description: Replace NavbarLinks with a NavbarSearch slot when search is more important than nav links. Pair with a Combobox or Input.

withUserMenu.label: With user dropdown
withUserMenu.description: Bell icon + an Avatar dropdown for account actions (Profile / Settings / Sign out). The dropdown is a separate DropdownMenu.

sticky.label: Sticky
sticky.description: Pass sticky to pin the Navbar at the top of the scroll container. Demo container scrolls — try scrolling it.

mobileCollapsed.label: Mobile collapsed
mobileCollapsed.description: Below md, NavbarLinks hide automatically. Add a menu Button (your own Sheet or Dialog trigger) that's only visible on mobile.

props.title: API
props.description: Six composition primitives. Drop them in any order; the trailing slot uses ms-auto to push right.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Navbar renders as <header> with semantic landmarks underneath. The hard parts are mobile-collapsed states and the skip-link pattern.

items.landmark: Navbar is a <header>; NavbarLinks renders as <nav>. Screen readers list both as landmarks. Don't wrap the page in a second <header> or <nav>.
items.current: Mark the current page on NavbarLink via active — sets aria-current="page" automatically. Pair with your router's NavLink for live sync.
items.mobile: Below md the NavbarLinks slot hides. Always provide an equivalent mobile path (Sheet, Dialog) — never leave keyboard users without a nav.
items.skipLink: Add a "Skip to main content" link as the very first child of <body> for keyboard users. Visually hidden until focused, then prominent.
items.search: If using NavbarSearch with an Input, apply a proper Label (sr-only is fine). A naked search field with just a placeholder is a common a11y miss.

dataTable.title: Data Table
dataTable.description: The most-used surface in the library — sortable, filterable, paginated tables backed by TanStack Table. Use for any data list with more than a handful of rows: tickets, customers, transactions, audit logs.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: 12 service-portal tickets across status / priority / assignee with global search and pagination.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the DataTable component plus optional helpers (createSelectColumn for the checkbox column, ColumnDef type from TanStack).

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a typed Ticket table with a sortable Subject column and a status Badge.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common configurations. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Six columns, no sorting yet. Toolbar + pagination come for free.

sortable.label: Sortable columns
sortable.description: Set enableSorting: true on each column. Headers become clickable; the primitive renders the chevron indicator automatically.

globalSearch.label: Global search
globalSearch.description: The toolbar's search box filters across every column. Try typing a customer name, ticket id, or status word.

filterSlot.label: With custom filter slot
filterSlot.description: Pass a Select / Combobox into filterSlot for column-specific filters. Manage the filtering in your own state and pass a pre-filtered data array.

rowSelection.label: With row selection
rowSelection.description: createSelectColumn() returns a column with header + per-row checkboxes. Drives TanStack's row selection model; read selected rows via table state.

rowActions.label: With row actions
rowActions.description: Render a DropdownMenu in a final column. enableSorting: false on the actions column so its header isn't clickable.

withAdd.label: With Add button
withAdd.description: Pass onAdd to render a + button at the end of the toolbar — the canonical "add new" affordance for table-based pages.

empty.label: Empty state
empty.description: An empty data array shows the built-in "No results." row. For better UX, render EmptyState beside or above the table with a primary action.

loading.label: Loading skeleton
loading.description: While fetching, render a skeleton-row Table that matches your real columns. Keep header content visible so the table doesn't reflow on load.

props.title: API
props.description: DataTable is a generic — it preserves your row type through columns and selection. The most useful props plus the helpers:

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: DataTable renders semantic <table> markup with proper header / body / row roles. The hard parts are sort announcements, row-selection semantics, and empty-state announcements.

items.semantics: Always use TableHead for column headers, not styled TableCells. Screen readers announce "row 3 column 4: Resolved" only when the markup is real <th>.
items.sort: Sorted column headers should signal direction via aria-sort. The primitive's chevron is visual only — for full SR support, add aria-sort="ascending" / "descending" / "none" in your column definition's header renderer.
items.selection: Per-row checkboxes need an aria-label like "Select row" or "Select REQ-2025-0142". createSelectColumn() does this automatically. Custom select columns must label both header and cell checkboxes.
items.actions: Row action triggers (the MoreHorizontal icon button) need aria-label including the row's identifier — e.g. "Actions for REQ-2025-0142". Otherwise screen readers announce "More button" 50 times.
items.empty: The built-in "No results." row reads as plain text. For meaningful empty states, render EmptyState — it carries a heading, body, and action with proper structure.

spinner.title: Spinner
spinner.description: An indeterminate loading indicator. Use when you don't know how long the wait is and there's nothing to skeleton. For known-progress operations (file upload, batch import), reach for Progress instead.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Default spinner — size md, primary tone.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a 'Loading tickets…' indicator inline with text.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default
default.description: Bare spinner. Decorative by default — set the label prop to expose it to screen readers.

sizes.label: Sizes
sizes.description: Five sizes from 12px (xs) to 32px (xl). Pair size with the surrounding text size.

tones.label: Tones
tones.description: Color via the tone prop. Use onPrimary when the spinner sits on a primary-colored surface (Button).

inlineWithText.label: Inline with text
inlineWithText.description: Small + muted, paired with a status message. The classic 'Loading…' pattern.

centered.label: Centered in container
centered.description: Vertically + horizontally centered inside a flex container during a section-level load.

onButton.label: On a button
onButton.description: Replace the button label with the spinner during an in-flight action. Disable the button to prevent double-submission.

overlay.label: Full-page overlay
overlay.description: For critical async transitions (auth check, route guard). Use sparingly — blocking the whole page is heavy.

accessibility.label: With accessible label
accessibility.description: When the spinner is the only signal that something's happening, set label to expose it as a role='status' live region.

props.title: API
props.description: Props available on the Spinner component.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Spinner is a decorative SVG by default. The hard parts are announcing the loading state to non-visual users and respecting reduced motion.

items.decorative: Without a label, the spinner is aria-hidden — screen readers ignore it. That's correct when the surrounding text already says 'Loading…'.
items.label: When the spinner is the only loading signal (e.g. on a Save button), set label so the SVG becomes a role='status' live region with an accessible name.
items.motion: The spin animation is a CSS transform via Tailwind's animate-spin — respects prefers-reduced-motion automatically (Tailwind disables animations under that media query).
items.duration: Show a spinner only for non-trivial waits (~200ms+). For sub-200ms operations, no indicator is calmer than a flash of spinner.
items.skeleton: Spinners are not skeletons. If you know the shape of the eventual content, use Skeleton — it gives users a sense of layout while loading.

progress.title: Progress
progress.description: A horizontal bar showing how much of a task is complete. Use for file uploads, batch operations, onboarding completion, and SLA countdowns. For indeterminate waits (no percent known), reach for Spinner.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Determinate progress bar at 68%.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a file upload bar with a label + percentage readout.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

determinate.label: Determinate
determinate.description: Bare bar at a fixed percent. Always pair with a label and percent readout in real UI.

withLabel.label: With label + value
withLabel.description: Two-column header above the bar — task name on the left, percent on the right. The canonical CRM pattern.

sizes.label: Sizes
sizes.description: Override height via className. h-1 for dense inline bars, h-3 (default) for most surfaces, h-5 for prominent dashboard tiles.

tones.label: Tones
tones.description: Target the inner indicator div with [&>div]:bg-* to tint. Use success/warning/error semantically (e.g. SLA proximity).

segmented.label: Segmented / stacked
segmented.description: Manually compose a flex container with multiple colored segments. The percent split shows category breakdown (resolved / in-progress / open).

liveUpload.label: Live upload simulation
liveUpload.description: Controlled value via setTimeout. Click Reset to replay the upload.

slaCountdown.label: SLA countdown — tone shifts at thresholds
slaCountdown.description: Three states (42% / 67% / 88%) showing how tone escalates as the deadline approaches. The classic 'getting urgent' pattern.

indeterminate.label: Indeterminate
indeterminate.description: Progress doesn't ship a true indeterminate mode. For quick 'in progress' feel, pulse the indicator. For a sliding stripe, define keyframes (see the code).

props.title: API
props.description: Props are forwarded to the Radix Progress root.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Radix Progress renders role="progressbar" with aria-valuenow / aria-valuemin / aria-valuemax. The hard parts are labeling and announcing completion.

items.value: Always pass a value (number) for determinate progress — screen readers announce it as 'progressbar, 68 percent'. Without a value, the role is still progressbar but the announcement is just 'in progress'.
items.label: Pair the bar with a visible label (the task name) and a screen-reader-accessible name via aria-labelledby. The percent readout is a sighted convenience; the label is the canonical name.
items.color: Tone changes (green / amber / red) carry meaning — pair with text or an icon so colorblind users get the urgency. Color alone fails WCAG 1.4.1.
items.live: If progress updates frequently (every <500ms), wrap the readout in aria-live="polite" so screen readers don't announce every increment.
items.indeterminate: The pulse / sliding-stripe patterns are visual only. For non-visual users, render an adjacent 'Processing…' status in an aria-live region so they know something's happening.

alert.title: Alert
alert.description: An inline notification that sits within page content — distinct from Banner (page-level, full-width) and Toast (transient, screen-edge). Use for state changes the user is already on the page to see: SLA risks, validation summaries, system messages tied to a specific section.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Info-tone alert with icon, title, and description.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Alert primitive and its slot helpers.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an SLA-breach-risk warning.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

variants.label: Five variants
variants.description: default (neutral card tint), info, success, warning, destructive. Status variants carry semantic meaning — don't use them as decorative accents.

withIcon.label: With icon
withIcon.description: Leading Lucide icon sits in a grid column to the left of the title. The primitive sizes svgs to 16px automatically.

titleOnly.label: Title only
titleOnly.description: Skip AlertDescription for short confirmations. The icon + title is enough context.

descriptionOnly.label: Description only
descriptionOnly.description: Skip AlertTitle for one-line system messages. The description carries the weight.

withAction.label: With action button
withAction.description: AlertAction renders absolutely at top-right. The primitive auto-pads the body so the action doesn't collide with text. Best for single-step remediation.

dismissible.label: Dismissible
dismissible.description: Mount/unmount via local state. The primitive doesn't ship a built-in close — use AlertAction with an icon button and your own onClick.

richContent.label: With inline link
richContent.description: AlertDescription supports anchors with automatic underline styling. Use for 'Resend' / 'Retry' / 'Learn more' affordances inside the body.

props.title: API
props.description: Four composition primitives — Alert + AlertTitle + AlertDescription + AlertAction.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Alert renders role="alert" via the primitive — screen readers announce the content when it first appears. The hard parts are choosing the right variant and not abusing the role.

items.role: role="alert" is a polite live region. Don't render multiple alerts on mount unless you want every one announced — assistive tech will read them sequentially.
items.semantic: Variant colors must match meaning. "success" green for completion, "warning" amber for caution, "destructive" red for errors. Swapping these breaks the colour-to-state mapping users learn.
items.icon: Icons reinforce the variant but shouldn't carry the only meaning. Always include text content — colour-blind users see the icon, not the colour.
items.dismiss: If dismissible, the close button needs aria-label="Dismiss" (or the action it performs). A naked X icon is invisible to screen readers without it.
items.choice: Pick Alert for in-context messages, Banner for page-level announcements (downtime, billing), Toast for transient confirmations. Three different surfaces, different priorities.

popover.title: Popover
popover.description: A floating panel anchored to a trigger. Click to open, click outside or Escape to close. Use for quick-edit fields, filter panels, compact action lists — anything richer than a Tooltip but lighter than a Dialog.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Default popover with a title + description.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a quick-edit field for a customer name.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common variants and patterns. Click the trigger areas to open each popover.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Default placement (bottom, center). PopoverTitle + PopoverDescription give standard text shape.

placements.label: Placements
placements.description: side + align cover the 12 placement combinations. The primitive flips automatically near viewport edges.

quickEdit.label: Quick edit
quickEdit.description: Embed an Input inside the popover for inline-edit-without-leaving-the-page patterns. The canonical CRM cell editor.

filterPanel.label: Filter panel
filterPanel.description: Multi-checkbox filter group with an Apply action. Wider content (w-72) sized to the controls inside.

controlled.label: Controlled open state
controlled.description: Drive open from React state. Needed when the popover state syncs with URL params, parent state, or external triggers.

customOffset.label: Custom offset
customOffset.description: sideOffset adds vertical/horizontal gap; alignOffset shifts along the alignment axis. Fine-tune when default positioning doesn't feel right.

iconTrigger.label: Icon-button trigger
iconTrigger.description: Compact icon trigger with a small action list inside. Common pattern in table rows + dense toolbars.

props.title: API
props.description: Composition primitive — props split across Popover root, Trigger, and Content.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Radix Popover ships proper ARIA roles, focus management, and keyboard navigation. The hard parts are labeling the trigger and choosing modal vs non-modal.

items.keyboard: Enter or Space on the trigger opens; Escape closes. Tab moves between focusable elements inside the content. Don't intercept the defaults.
items.focus: When the popover opens, focus moves to the first focusable element inside the content. On close, focus returns to the trigger automatically.
items.label: Icon-only triggers need aria-label. A naked button with just an icon reads as 'button' to screen readers — useless without context.
items.modal: modal=true traps focus and renders an inert backdrop. Reserve for popovers that require explicit confirm. Most popovers should stay non-modal (default).
items.touch: Click-outside dismissal works on touch via pointer events. For richer touch UI (drag handles, swipe), use Drawer instead.

hoverCard.title: Hover Card
hoverCard.description: A popover triggered by hover, typically for previews — assignee details, ticket summaries, link previews. Distinct from Tooltip (text-only, short delay, no rich content) and Popover (click-triggered).

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Hover the @amal-hassan mention to see a compact assignee preview.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Hover Card primitives. Built on @radix-ui/react-hover-card.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an assignee preview card.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common patterns. Hover the triggers below to open each card.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Bare trigger + content. Use for quick text previews where Tooltip is too small but Popover is too clicky.

userPreview.label: User preview
userPreview.description: Avatar + name + role + contact details. The canonical 'hover assignee' pattern in comments and assignment dropdowns.

ticketPreview.label: Ticket / link preview
ticketPreview.description: Hover a ticket reference in a comment to see status, customer, SLA. Saves a round-trip click into the ticket detail page.

delayTuning.label: Delay tuning
delayTuning.description: openDelay + closeDelay control hover sensitivity. Shorter for quick references (~150ms); longer when hover is incidental and false-opens would be noisy.

placements.label: Placements
placements.description: Same side / align controls as Popover. Hover Card auto-flips near viewport edges.

props.title: API
props.description: Composition primitive — three pieces.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Hover Card is hover-only by design — that's its strength and its weakness. The hard parts are touch fallback and keyboard support.

items.touch: Touch devices don't have hover. Hover Card content is inaccessible on mobile by default. For information that must be reachable on touch, add a Popover trigger as well — or restructure so the data is on-screen.
items.essential: Never put essential information in a Hover Card. If users need it to complete a task, the data belongs in the page, a Popover (clickable), or a dedicated detail view.
items.keyboard: Focusing the trigger via Tab also opens the card. That's the Radix default — don't disable it. It's the only keyboard path to the content.
items.delay: Match openDelay to the content's purpose. Quick references (~150–300ms) feel responsive; rich previews (~500–800ms) avoid false-opens when scanning past triggers.
items.alternative: For status indicators or short labels, use Tooltip — it has built-in screen-reader support via aria-describedby. Hover Card is for richer content that wouldn't fit in a Tooltip.

drawer.title: Drawer / Sheet
drawer.description: Slide-out panels from a screen edge. Per Step 2's nav consolidation, this one docs page covers two distinct primitives — pick Sheet for desktop side panels (Radix Dialog), Drawer for mobile drag-to-dismiss (vaul).

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Sheet (right) and Drawer (bottom) side-by-side so you can feel the physics difference.

intro.title: Two primitives, one docs page
intro.body: Despite the shared docs surface, Sheet and Drawer are different components with different physics. The Step 2 consolidation was nav-level — pick the one that fits your use case.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import from whichever primitive matches your use case — or both if a single page needs desktop sheet + mobile drawer.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an edit-customer sheet from the right.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common shapes. Click triggers to open each panel.

sheetRight.label: Sheet from right — quick preview
sheetRight.description: The default Sheet placement. Best for desktop quick-preview panels that don't need full focus.

sheetLeft.label: Sheet from left — mobile menu
sheetLeft.description: Mobile-style hamburger menu. side='left' + a narrow max-width.

sheetWithForm.label: Sheet with header + scrollable body + footer
sheetWithForm.description: The canonical edit-detail pattern. Header is fixed, body scrolls, footer pins to the bottom with the primary action.

drawerBottom.label: Drawer from bottom — filter panel
drawerBottom.description: Mobile-feeling drag-to-dismiss. The vaul drag handle is visible at the top; swipe down or tap outside to close.

drawerDirection.label: Drawer directions
drawerDirection.description: Drawer supports bottom (default), right, left, top via the direction prop on the Root. Each direction gets edge-appropriate styling.

sizes.label: Sheet sizes
sizes.description: Override width via sm:max-w-* on SheetContent. sm/md/lg/xl cover the standard widths.

props.title: API
props.description: Two composition primitives — same shape, different physics underneath.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Both primitives handle focus trap, restoration, and keyboard navigation via their respective libraries. The hard parts are titles + touch + choosing the right primitive.

items.focus: Both Sheet and Drawer trap focus inside the panel while open. On close, focus returns to the trigger automatically — don't override.
items.keyboard: Escape closes both. Tab moves between focusable elements inside. Sheet adds an X close button by default; Drawer relies on the drag handle + click outside.
items.title: Always include SheetTitle / DrawerTitle. Both libraries wire it as the aria-labelledby of the panel — without it, screen readers announce 'dialog' with no context.
items.touch: Drawer is touch-first (drag handle, momentum). Sheet works on touch but doesn't ship the drag affordance. For mobile bottom panels, prefer Drawer.
items.choice: Sheet for desktop side panels where the user is mouse/keyboard-driven. Drawer for mobile bottom sheets where touch physics matter. Dialog for centred modals demanding explicit confirm.

aspectRatio.title: Aspect Ratio
aspectRatio.description: A ratio-constrained container. Forces children into a fixed width-to-height proportion regardless of available space. Use for thumbnails, video embeds, attachment grids — anywhere uniform proportions matter.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: 16:9 ratio — the canonical video / hero proportion.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the AspectRatio primitive. Built on @radix-ui/react-aspect-ratio.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a customer photo with a square crop.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common ratios. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

widescreen.label: 16 : 9 widescreen
widescreen.description: Video and hero banner proportions. The most common landscape ratio.

classic.label: 4 : 3 classic
classic.description: Traditional photo proportion. Less wide than 16:9 — good for compact thumbnails.

square.label: 1 : 1 square
square.description: Avatars, thumbnail grids, icon containers. The neutral choice when content shape varies.

portrait.label: 3 : 4 portrait
portrait.description: Profile cards, vertical previews, mobile-first hero images.

customRatio.label: Custom ratios
customRatio.description: Any number works. 21:9 for ultrawide banners, 2:1 for hero strips. Pass the literal division — Radix normalizes.

attachmentGrid.label: Attachment grid
attachmentGrid.description: Force consistent thumbnail proportions in a multi-attachment cell. The grid stays clean regardless of source image dimensions.

props.title: API
props.description: One prop — ratio. The container fills its parent's width and computes height accordingly.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: AspectRatio is a layout wrapper — accessibility comes from what you put inside it.

items.alt: When wrapping , always set alt. Empty alt='' is correct for decorative thumbnails; meaningful text is needed for content images.
items.layout: The ratio container doesn't add a role or landmark. Wrap with <figure> or another semantic element if the content needs one.
items.media: For <video> or <iframe>, set the aria-label on the inner element — the ratio wrapper carries no semantic value.

separator.title: Separator
separator.description: A horizontal or vertical divider line. Use to break up sections, separate menu groups, or split toolbar regions.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Horizontal separator between two text rows.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a customer card with a section break.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

horizontal.label: Horizontal
horizontal.description: Default orientation. Full width of the parent, 1px tall.

vertical.label: Vertical
vertical.description: Set orientation='vertical' — pair with a flex parent so the separator stretches to its siblings' height.

withLabel.label: With center label
withLabel.description: Compose two separators flanking a small text node. The classic 'OR' divider in auth forms.

inMenu.label: Inside a menu
inMenu.description: Separate destructive or unrelated items from the main group. The most common Separator placement.

inToolbar.label: Vertical in a toolbar
inToolbar.description: Group related toolbar buttons with vertical separators between groups. Set an explicit height (e.g. h-5) since the toolbar itself is small.

inCard.label: Inside a card
inCard.description: Section break inside a content card. The most common Separator placement after menus.

props.title: API
props.description: Three props — orientation, decorative, and className.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Separator is built on base-ui. The hard part is choosing decorative vs semantic correctly.

items.heading: Don't use Separator as a section heading replacement. Headings (h2/h3/etc.) carry hierarchy; separators are just visual rhythm.
items.vertical: Vertical separators need a parent with a defined height (via flex stretch or h-*). Without one, they collapse to zero height — invisible.
items.role: Separator renders with role="separator" via base-ui. For purely visual rhythm with no semantic break, wrap or hide the separator yourself with aria-hidden — most callsites should leave the default.

container.title: Container
container.description: A max-width centred wrapper with responsive horizontal padding. The page-level layout primitive — wrap your route content in one Container, then compose Stack / Grid inside.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Default size='lg' — 1024px max width with px-4 sm:px-6 lg:px-8 padding.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a page root rendered as <main> with the xl breakpoint.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common sizes + composition. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

default.label: Default (lg)
default.description: Most pages use size='lg'. 1024px is wide enough for dashboards, narrow enough to keep line lengths comfortable.

sizes.label: Sizes
sizes.description: Six widths mapping to Tailwind's max-w-screen-* (sm=640, md=768, lg=1024, xl=1280, 2xl=1536) + 'full' for viewport-hugging layouts.

customPadding.label: Custom padding
customPadding.description: Override the default px-4 sm:px-6 lg:px-8 via className. Use for edge-to-edge sections (px-0) or denser layouts.

asElement.label: Render as semantic element
asElement.description: Pass as='main' / 'section' / 'article' for proper page landmarks. Each route should have one <main> Container at the top level.

nested.label: Nested containers
nested.description: Wrap a narrower Container inside a wider one for prose blocks (md for article body) inside a wide page (2xl for dashboards).

props.title: API
props.description: Three props: size, as, and className.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Container is a layout wrapper — the accessibility concerns are semantic landmarks and reading width.

items.semantics: Pass as='main' for the page root so screen readers announce a main landmark. Each page needs exactly one. Use 'section' or 'article' for nested blocks with their own headings.
items.lineLength: Wider isn't always better. Prose at size='md' (768px) reads more comfortably than at 'xl' (1280px) — the eye gets lost on long lines. Match container width to content type.
items.padding: The default padding stays consistent at px-4 on mobile, px-6 sm, px-8 lg. Don't shrink below px-4 — small viewports become claustrophobic with no breathing room.

collapsible.title: Collapsible
collapsible.description: A single section that toggles open and closed. Use for one-off reveals — internal notes, FAQ items, optional form fields. For a group of mutually-exclusive sections, use Accordion.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Click the trigger to expand/collapse the content.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the Collapsible primitives. Built on @radix-ui/react-collapsible.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — collapsible internal notes on a ticket detail.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Common patterns. Click triggers to toggle each section.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Bare trigger + content. No chevron, no styling — just toggle behavior.

withChevron.label: With chevron indicator
withChevron.description: Add a chevron that rotates on open/close via the data-[state] selectors. The canonical visual.

defaultOpen.label: Default open
defaultOpen.description: Start expanded when the content is primary information that should be visible by default.

controlled.label: Controlled
controlled.description: Drive open state from React. Useful when toggling from outside the primitive (URL param sync, external buttons).

withCustomTrigger.label: Custom trigger
withCustomTrigger.description: Render the trigger as a Button (or any element) via asChild. Keeps the existing button styling and behaviour.

ticketDetail.label: Inside a card
ticketDetail.description: Real-world CRM pattern — a collapsible 'Internal notes' section inside a ticket detail card.

props.title: API
props.description: Three composition primitives. Root + Trigger + Content.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Radix Collapsible handles ARIA attributes and keyboard navigation. The hard parts are choosing it vs Accordion and labeling icon-only triggers.

items.keyboard: Enter or Space on the trigger toggles the content. Tab moves to the next focusable element. Standard Radix behavior — don't override.
items.aria: Trigger gets aria-expanded automatically; Content gets aria-hidden when closed. Screen readers announce the open/close state on every toggle.
items.accordion: If you have multiple sections where opening one should close others, use Accordion (type='single'). Collapsible is for one-off reveals; managing several independently means re-implementing Accordion poorly.

resizable.title: Resizable
resizable.description: Drag-to-resize panels with snap, min/max, and persisted layouts. Use for IDE-style workspaces, email clients, and ticket-list-plus-detail views where users want to control their layout.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Horizontal pair with the default thin handle. Hover the divider and drag to resize.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the three composition primitives. Built on react-resizable-panels.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a ticket list on the left, detail on the right, both resizable.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Directions, visible handles, size limits, and CRM compositions. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

horizontal.label: Horizontal
horizontal.description: Default direction — side-by-side panels. The handle is a 1px line that's easier to discover with withHandle.

vertical.label: Vertical
vertical.description: Stack panels top-to-bottom. Useful for editor-plus-output layouts or split content above a log.

withHandle.label: Visible handle
withHandle.description: Pass withHandle to render a small grip in the middle of the divider. The default 1px line is hard to find without it.

minMax.label: Min / max size
minMax.description: Clamp how small or large a panel can get. Especially important for sidebars — set minSize so users can't accidentally hide them entirely.

threePanel.label: Three-pane (CRM)
threePanel.description: Real-world CRM pattern — folders / conversations / message, all resizable. The canonical email-app layout.

nested.label: Nested groups
nested.description: Nest a vertical group inside a horizontal one for IDE-style layouts (sidebar + editor-with-terminal).

props.title: API
props.description: ResizablePanelGroup + ResizablePanel + ResizableHandle. Sizes are percentages of the group.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Drag-only handles are an a11y problem — react-resizable-panels solves it with keyboard support and ARIA, but you still need to make the handle discoverable.

items.keyboard: The handle is focusable. Arrow keys move it by ~1% per keystroke, Shift+Arrow by ~10%. Home/End jump to min/max. Don't remove the focus ring.
items.handle: Pass withHandle in production. The 1px default handle is invisible to mouse users who don't already know it's there — and impossible for low-vision users to find.
items.touch: Touch targets are wider than the visual handle (the after: pseudo-element). Don't override that without keeping a 24px+ hit area.

accordion.title: Accordion
accordion.description: A vertically stacked set of headings that each reveal a section of content. Use for FAQs, settings sections, and any list where users open one panel at a time to focus on its content.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Rounded variant with two FAQ items. Click any trigger to expand its content.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — an internal FAQ inside a card.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Visual variants, multi-open, controlled state, and CRM patterns. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

transparent.label: Transparent
transparent.description: Bordered list — the default. Best placed inside a card so the borders don't float on the page background.

rounded.label: Rounded
rounded.description: Each item becomes a self-contained card with background and rounded corners. Use when the accordion is a top-level surface, not nested in another card.

multiple.label: Multiple open
multiple.description: Pass type='multiple' to let users open several panels at once. Useful for settings where related sections may need to be visible together.

defaultOpen.label: Default open
defaultOpen.description: Start with one or more items expanded via defaultValue. Use when the first item contains primary content that should be visible immediately.

controlled.label: Controlled
controlled.description: Drive open state from React. Useful when you need to react to changes, sync to a URL, or open panels from external buttons.

ticketFaq.label: Onboarding FAQ
ticketFaq.description: Real-world CRM pattern — new-agent onboarding FAQ inside a labelled card. Rounded variant gives each Q&A its own visual block.

props.title: API
props.description: Accordion + AccordionItem + AccordionTrigger + AccordionContent. Built on base-ui's Accordion primitive.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Base-ui handles ARIA wiring; your job is choosing accordion vs. tabs vs. collapsible, and ensuring trigger labels stand alone.

items.keyboard: Enter or Space on a trigger toggles its panel. Arrow Up/Down moves focus between triggers. Home / End jump to the first / last. Tab moves out of the accordion entirely.
items.aria: Trigger gets aria-expanded; Panel gets aria-hidden when closed and the matching ID linkage. Don't override these — the underlying primitive needs them intact.
items.collapsible: For a single, one-off reveal (not a group of related sections), Collapsible is the right primitive. For mutually exclusive views with no need to collapse all, consider Tabs.

stack.title: Stack
stack.description: A flex wrapper for one-dimensional layouts — vertical (VStack) or horizontal (HStack). The primitive for spacing children in a row or column with consistent gaps. Optional dividers between children.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: VStack with gap='3' — three items stacked vertically with consistent spacing.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import Stack and its directional aliases.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a ticket header with title block on the left and action buttons on the right.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Vertical, horizontal, alignment, dividers, wrapping, and CRM compositions. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

vstack.label: VStack
vstack.description: Vertical stack with consistent gap between children. Use for card lists, form sections, anything that flows top-to-bottom.

hstack.label: HStack
hstack.description: Horizontal stack. Defaults to align='center' since most horizontal rows want vertical centering. Common for button rows and toolbar segments.

alignJustify.label: Align + justify
alignJustify.description: Pair justify='between' with align='center' for header bars — title on the left, actions on the right. The most common HStack pattern.

divider.label: With dividers
divider.description: Pass a divider prop (typically <Separator />) and Stack renders one between each pair of children — not before the first or after the last.

wrap.label: Wrapping
wrap.description: Set wrap when children overflow horizontally — tag rows, chip lists. Without it the items shrink or clip.

asElement.label: Render as ul
asElement.description: When stacking list items, pass as='ul' and use <li> children for correct semantics.

ticketRow.label: Ticket list row
ticketRow.description: Real-world CRM pattern — vertical list of ticket rows, each row an HStack with subject/meta on the left and a priority badge on the right.

props.title: API
props.description: Stack + HStack + VStack share the same props. HStack defaults to direction='row' and align='center'; VStack defaults to direction='column'.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Stack is a layout primitive — the a11y concerns are semantics, source order, and how dividers are announced.

items.semantics: Pass as='ul' / 'ol' when children are list items, 'section' with aria-labelledby for labeled regions. A bare <div> is fine for unlabeled visual groupings.
items.order: Flex direction affects visual order, not source order. Source order is what screen readers and keyboard users follow — don't use 'row-reverse' or order: -1 for primary content placement.
items.dividers: The Separator divider is decorative by default. If you need it announced as a semantic boundary (between distinct regions), use a Separator with appropriate role or wrap regions in <section> instead.

grid.title: Grid
grid.description: A CSS Grid wrapper with column-count and gap tokens — the primitive for laying out KPI rows, dashboard widgets, and any equally-spaced multi-column composition.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Three equal columns with the default gap. Cells size themselves to fill their track.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a row of three KPI cards above a dashboard.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Column counts, gap scale, responsive overrides, and the 12-column variant. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

basic.label: Basic
basic.description: Three columns, default gap. The most common dashboard pattern.

responsive.label: Responsive
responsive.description: Start at 1 column on mobile, 2 at md, 4 at lg. Use Tailwind's responsive prefixes via className — Grid's cols prop sets the default.

twelveCol.label: 12-column
twelveCol.description: Pass cols='12' and use col-span-* on children for asymmetric layouts (main + sidebar, holy grail, etc).

gaps.label: Gap scale
gaps.description: Tokens 0–12 map to Tailwind's gap-* utilities. Override gap-x-* / gap-y-* via className for axis-specific gaps.

asElement.label: Render as ul
asElement.description: When the grid is semantically a list (team members, tags, attachments), pass as='ul' and use <li> children.

ticketBoard.label: Ticket board
ticketBoard.description: Real-world CRM pattern — a three-column Kanban board with stacked ticket cards in each column.

props.title: API
props.description: Five props: cols, gap, as, className, children.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Grid is a visual layout — the a11y concerns are semantics and source order, not the grid itself.

items.semantics: Pass as='ul' / 'ol' when the cells are list items, 'section' with an aria-labelledby for labeled regions. A bare <div> is fine for unlabeled visual groupings.
items.order: CSS Grid lets you visually reorder children, but the source order is what screen readers and keyboard users follow. Keep DOM order matching reading order — don't rely on grid-auto-flow or order: -1 for primary content.
items.responsive: Collapsing from 4 columns down to 1 on mobile changes the visual order; double-check that the source order still makes sense when laid out as a single stack.

scrollArea.title: Scroll Area
scrollArea.description: A scrollable region with styled scrollbars that match the design system instead of the browser default. Use anywhere content might overflow a fixed-height container — sidebars, long lists, conversation threads.

preview.title: Preview
preview.description: Vertical scroll with a custom thumb. Scrollbars appear on hover; the viewport handles wheel and touch.

installation.title: Installation
installation.description: Import the ScrollArea primitive. Built on @radix-ui/react-scroll-area.

usage.title: Usage
usage.description: Smallest viable real-world snippet — a fixed-height list of tickets that scrolls.

examples.title: Examples
examples.description: Vertical, horizontal, both axes, and CRM compositions. Toggle "View code" on any preview to copy the snippet.

vertical.label: Vertical
vertical.description: The default. Set a max-height on the ScrollArea and let the viewport handle overflow.

horizontal.label: Horizontal
horizontal.description: Add a horizontal ScrollBar for tag/chip rows or thumbnail strips. The viewport content must be wider than the container.

both.label: Both axes
both.description: Render both scrollbars for wide tables or canvas-like content. The corner element bridges where the two scrollbars meet.

customThumb.label: Custom thumb
customThumb.description: Override the thumb classes for a thicker bar or branded color. The default uses border / muted-foreground tokens.

conversation.label: Conversation thread
conversation.description: Real-world CRM pattern — a fixed-height ticket conversation that scrolls inside a card without breaking the page layout.

tagRow.label: Horizontal tag row
tagRow.description: Customer tags that overflow horizontally. ScrollArea keeps the row a single line without browser-native scrollbars cluttering the UI.

props.title: API
props.description: ScrollArea wraps Radix's Root + Viewport + ScrollBar + Corner. Use ScrollBar directly only when you need a horizontal axis or both.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.intro: Custom scrollbars are a tricky a11y area — Radix handles most of it, but you still need to make the viewport reachable by keyboard.

items.keyboard: The viewport is focusable so keyboard users can scroll with arrow keys, Page Up/Down, Home/End. Don't remove the focus ring — the default ring-2 ring-ring is intentional.
items.wheel: Mouse wheel and touch gestures route to the viewport automatically. Don't intercept them with extra wrappers or onWheel handlers.
items.fallback: If a user prefers native scrollbars (reduce-motion, system preference), the underlying viewport still scrolls. The custom UI is purely visual.

log.submittedDesc: Request was successfully received by the system
log.idVerifiedDesc: Client identity verified via UAE PASS

colors.title: Colors
colors.description: The color system has two layers: semantic tokens that adapt to light and dark mode (the contract apps consume), and a fixed palette of brand and status scales (the raw ingredients).

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: Components use semantic tokens like --primary and --foreground, not raw palette values. This means a single override on a theme variable cascades to every component that consumes it — and light/dark mode swap automatically. The palette below shows the raw scales the semantic tokens are built from.

semantic.title: Semantic tokens
semantic.description: These are the surfaces components use. Each row shows the resolved value in light and dark mode side by side, regardless of your current theme.

palette.title: Palette
palette.description: Every fixed brand, status, neutral, and accent scale in the system. Values are identical in light and dark mode — modes differ only in which shade the semantic tokens point at.
palette.hint: Click a swatch to copy var(--color-name). Shift+click to copy the Tailwind class (bg-name-shade).

opacity.title: Adjusting opacity
opacity.description: Append /N to any color utility to apply N% opacity. Useful for hover backgrounds, disabled states, and tinted surfaces.
opacity.note: Syntax also accepts arbitrary values (bg-primary/[37%]) and CSS variables (bg-primary/(--my-alpha)).

typography.title: Typography
typography.description: Two type families — Dubai for UI body, Bukra for display and metadata — paired with a 9-step semantic scale that maps to Tailwind utility classes.

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: Type comes in two faces. Dubai is the workhorse: body copy, labels, table cells, anything that needs to be read at length. Bukra carries the brand: display headings, button text, badges, monospace metadata. The size scale uses Tailwind defaults — semantic names (H1, Body, Caption) map to text-* utilities so the same names work whether you're writing JSX, snippets, or design specs.

families.title: Font families
families.description: Each face is exposed as a CSS variable and a Tailwind utility class. Weights below are the variants self-hosted in /public/fonts.

scale.title: Type scale
scale.description: Nine semantic steps from Display down to Caption. Each step is one Tailwind utility class — no custom sizes.

tailwind.title: Tailwind mapping
tailwind.description: Reach for the semantic name in design conversations; reach for the Tailwind class in code. Same scale, two surfaces.

rules.title: Usage rules
rules.description: Conventions that keep body copy readable and headings consistent.

items.lineLength: Cap body copy at ~65 characters per line (max-w-prose or max-w-3xl). Long lines are exhausting to scan, especially on wide screens.
items.lineHeight: Headings get tight line-height (1.0–1.25); body copy needs ~1.5. The values above are the right defaults — don't tighten body for visual density.
items.weight: Use weight to establish hierarchy, not size alone. A Medium label next to a Regular value reads more cleanly than two sizes side by side.
items.mono: font-mono is for metadata: IDs, amounts, timestamps, status codes, code. Don't use it for paragraphs — Bukra is hard to read at length.
items.alignment: Left-align body copy in LTR contexts (start-align with i18n). Justified text creates uneven word spacing — avoid except in print contexts.
items.scaling: Don't introduce sizes outside the scale. If the design calls for something between text-base and text-lg, pick one and adjust line-height or weight instead.

override.title: How to override
override.description: Swap brand fonts by overriding the CSS variables. Add @font-face declarations in your app stylesheet if you're self-hosting.

spacing.title: Spacing & Layout
spacing.description: A 4px base scale for padding, margin, and gaps, plus the standard container widths used across docs and dashboards. Built directly on Tailwind's spacing system — no custom variables to remember.

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: Every spacing utility — p-1, gap-4, m-8 — derives from a single base unit of 4px. Sticking to the scale keeps rhythm consistent across pages and components; one-off values like p-[13px] break the grid and accumulate visual noise. When a design feels 'off by a few pixels', the answer is usually to step up or down on the scale, not to invent a new number.

scale.title: Spacing scale
scale.description: The 17 most-used steps. Each utility class accepts the token as a suffix (p-4, gap-6, mt-12). The width column is rendered at the actual size — verify it's the spacing you remember.

containers.title: Container widths
containers.description: Tailwind's max-w-* utilities, paired with the typical use case. Most docs pages cap at max-w-6xl; prose content caps at max-w-prose.

tailwind.title: Tailwind mapping
tailwind.description: The spacing scale powers a family of utility classes. Same numeric suffix, different property.

rules.title: Usage rules
rules.description: Conventions that keep the grid honest.

items.scale: Stay on the scale. p-[13px] should be p-3 (12px) or p-4 (16px), not a one-off arbitrary value.
items.gapVsSpace: Prefer gap-* over space-x-* / space-y-* in new flex/grid containers. gap respects wrapping correctly and doesn't add extra margins to children.
items.responsive: Spacing scales with viewport. The common pattern is smaller-on-mobile, larger-on-desktop: p-4 lg:p-8, gap-3 md:gap-6. Don't pick the desktop value and hope it works on mobile.
items.logical: Use logical-axis utilities (ps-*, pe-*, ms-*, me-*) instead of physical (pl-*, pr-*, ml-*, mr-*) so the layout adapts to RTL languages without conditional CSS.
items.padVsMargin: Padding makes a component bigger; margin pushes it away from siblings. For consistent component sizing, prefer padding inside the component and let the parent control margin/gap.
items.prose: Long-form text needs max-w-prose. Wide paragraphs are exhausting to read — the cap exists for the reader, not the designer.

override.title: How to override
override.description: Adjust the base unit in your app stylesheet — the entire scale rescales proportionally.

radius.title: Border Radius
radius.description: A proportional radius scale anchored on a single --radius variable. Every other step is derived via calc(), so changing the base rescales the entire system.

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: The radius scale exists to make rounding feel intentional rather than arbitrary. Eight calc-derived steps from sm to 4xl plus none and full handle every interface need — inputs, cards, buttons, badges, avatars. The whole scale rises and falls with --radius; if the brand wants sharper or friendlier corners, change one value.

scale.title: Radius scale
scale.description: Each step has a CSS variable and a Tailwind class. The Shape column renders the actual radius applied to a sample square.

context.title: In context
context.description: Mixed radii on a real card — input uses md, buttons use lg, container uses 2xl, avatar and badge use full.
context.note: When you nest radii, the outer radius should always be larger than the inner one. A rounded-2xl card with rounded-md children reads as a single shape; mismatched radii read as a mistake.

tailwind.title: Tailwind mapping
tailwind.description: All steps are exposed as standard Tailwind rounded-* utilities.

items.consistency: Use the same radius for components of the same role. All buttons share one radius; all cards share another. Mixing radii inside a single role looks accidental.
items.nesting: Outer radius > inner radius. A rounded-2xl card with rounded-md inputs feels right; the reverse looks broken.
items.full: rounded-full is for pills (badges, capsule buttons) and circles (avatars). It's not a 'maximum round' — use it intentionally, not as a fallback when other sizes feel too small.
items.none: rounded-none is the right answer for table cells, sticky headers, and edge-to-edge content. Square corners aren't ugly — they're functional.
items.scale: Don't introduce rounded-[13px] one-offs. If md (8px) and lg (10px) feel wrong, the answer is to step up to xl (14px), not invent 13px.

override.title: How to override
override.description: Adjust --radius in your app stylesheet — the whole scale rescales proportionally.

motion.title: Motion
motion.description: Five duration tokens and six easing curves, tuned so transitions feel responsive without being twitchy. Built on real CSS variables — change one to rescale every animation in the system.

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: Motion exists to explain a change, not decorate it. A modal scales in so the user can see where it came from. A toast slides up so it doesn't appear from nowhere. A focus ring fades in so the keyboard user sees what they're on. Everything else — hover ripples, parallax, looping ambient animation — is decoration, and decoration ages badly. The tokens below cover the explanatory cases; reach for them, not arbitrary durations.
