overview.hint: Most components in this codebase animate with duration-normal (300ms) and --ease-custom — that's the safe default.

durations.title: Durations
durations.description: Five steps from instant to slower. Hover any row to play the duration on the ball. The right pick scales with what's moving: small things, fast; large things, slow.

easings.title: Easings
easings.description: Six curves. The first four are Tailwind defaults; --ease-custom and --ease-spring are project additions. Hover any row to play the easing. Pair with --duration-slow to feel the difference clearly.

reducedMotion.title: Reduced motion
reducedMotion.callout: The design system does not enforce prefers-reduced-motion automatically. Your application stylesheet must include the @media block — see the override snippet below.
reducedMotion.body: Users with vestibular disorders, attention disorders, or motion sensitivity set their OS to prefers-reduced-motion: reduce. The browser exposes that signal to CSS. Animations don't have to disappear entirely — collapse them to ~10ms so the state change still happens but the motion doesn't. Loops, scroll-jacking, and parallax should be removed outright in this mode.

items.purpose: Animate to explain a change in state or location. If you can't articulate what the motion is teaching, cut it.
items.duration: Small things move fast (badge, tooltip → 150ms). Large things move slow (modal, page transition → 500ms). The middle (300ms) covers most interactions.
items.easing: Use ease-out for entrances, ease-in for exits, ease-in-out for paired transitions. ease-linear is for continuous motion only (spinners, progress).
items.transform: Animate transform and opacity. Avoid animating layout properties (width, height, top, left, margin) — they trigger layout recalculation on every frame and stutter on slow devices.
items.spring: ease-spring overshoots. Use it for delight moments (success checkmark, badge pop-in) — not for everyday transitions, where it reads as wobbly.
items.state: Don't use motion as the only signal of a state change. A button that only animates on success leaves screen-reader users in the dark — pair with a text label, aria-live, or an icon.

override.title: How to override
override.description: All motion tokens are real CSS variables. Override them in your app stylesheet to retune the system globally, and include the prefers-reduced-motion fallback.

elevation.title: Elevation & Shadows
elevation.description: Two systems that work together: semantic box-shadows for visual lift, and z-index layers for stacking order. Both are first-class CSS variables — change one to retune the whole hierarchy.

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: Elevation is about hierarchy, not decoration. A card is slightly above the page; a popover is clearly floating; a modal commands attention. The same logic applies to z-index: dropdowns, modals, popovers, and toasts have a known stacking order so they don't fight each other. Use the semantic shadows for component-role lift, the Tailwind defaults for general-purpose elevation, and the z-index layers for any custom overlay you build.

semantic.title: Semantic shadows
semantic.description: Four intent-named shadows mapped to component roles. Reach for these in components you build (cards, popovers, modals, toasts) so elevation stays consistent across the system.

tailwind.title: Tailwind shadow scale
tailwind.description: Tailwind's built-in shadow utilities remain available for general-purpose elevation. Use them for buttons, badges, dense surfaces, or anywhere a semantic shadow feels too specific.

zindex.title: Z-index layers
zindex.description: Nine semantic layers with gaps of 10–20 between them. Apply via inline style (z-index: var(--z-modal)) or via Tailwind's arbitrary-value utility (z-[var(--z-modal)]).

items.intent: Match shadow to intent. A floating popover should not use shadow-card; a static card should not use shadow-modal. The visual depth communicates what the element is.
items.layers: Don't invent z-index numbers. If your component needs to stack above a modal but below a tooltip, slot it between --z-modal (1050) and --z-tooltip (1080) — pick 1055, document why.
items.dark: Dark-mode shadows usually need higher alpha to be visible against the dark background. Override --shadow-* values in .dark if the defaults feel weak.
items.adhoc: Avoid shadow-[0_5px_15px_rgba(...)] one-offs. If the existing scale doesn't fit, propose a new semantic token rather than scattering arbitrary values.
items.color: Shadows are color too — they pick up tone from the page. On colored or photographic backgrounds, a colored shadow (subtle hue-shift) reads more natural than pure black.

override.title: How to override
override.description: All shadow and z-index tokens are real CSS variables — override in your app stylesheet to retune the system globally, including dark-mode-specific values.

iconography.title: Iconography
iconography.description: Built on Lucide — a 1000+ icon set with consistent stroke weight and 24×24 geometry. Sized through Tailwind's size-* utilities, colored via currentColor, and labeled for accessibility wherever they stand alone.

overview.title: Overview
overview.body: All icons in the codebase come from lucide-react. Lucide gives us a single visual language across the dashboard, dark-mode-friendly stroke shapes, and tree-shaken imports — only the icons you import ship in your bundle. Pick from the gallery below, or browse the full set on lucide.dev.

library.title: Library
library.description: A sample of the icons used across the codebase. The full set has 1000+ glyphs — these eight are representative of the visual style and stroke weight.

sizes.title: Sizes
sizes.description: Four standard sizes covering every common need, from compact inline labels to display headings. Use Tailwind's size-* utility to set both width and height in one class.

uses.16: Inline with body text. Inside small buttons, dense table cells, badges.
uses.20: Default UI scale. Most icon buttons, navigation, dropdown items.
uses.24: Card headers, profile menus, larger interactive surfaces.
uses.32: Empty states, hero CTAs, page-level affordances.

stroke.title: Stroke width
stroke.description: Lucide icons accept a strokeWidth prop. Default is 2. Adjust per surface — light strokes for dense UIs, bold for emphasis.

color.title: Color
color.description: Icons inherit currentColor. Apply any text-* utility and the icon picks it up — no fill or stroke props needed.

patterns.title: Common patterns
patterns.description: How icons show up across the system. Each example uses tokens from Colors and Spacing — same primitives, different compositions.
patterns.iconText: Icon with text — aria-label is optional; the text labels the action.

items.ariaLabel: Icon-only buttons / links MUST have aria-label. Without it, a screen reader announces "button" with no context. "Settings", "Delete", "Open menu" — be specific.
items.ariaHidden: Decorative icons (icon paired with adjacent text) should have aria-hidden so the screen reader doesn't repeat the meaning twice. Search + "Search tickets" should be announced once, not twice.
items.sizing: Use size-* not h-* w-* separately. size-4 is more reliable than h-4 w-4 because both axes always match.
items.color: Color icons via text-* utilities, never via fill or stroke props. text-muted-foreground keeps decorative icons quiet; text-primary highlights interactive ones.
items.alignment: Icons next to text usually need flex items-center on the parent. Don't add vertical-align margins to the icon — let flex handle it.
items.consistency: Pick one icon per concept and reuse it. If "Edit" is a Pencil in one place and an Edit3 in another, users notice. The codebase already has favorites — search before you import a new one.

install.title: Using icons
install.description: Lucide is already installed (lucide-react). Import only the icons you need — tree-shaking handles the rest.
install.usageIntro: A realistic example — search input with a leading icon:
install.iconOnlyIntro: Icon-only buttons need an accessible name. Always:

animated.title: Animated icons
animated.description: A collection of green-tinted Lottie animations for moments that benefit from motion — KPI tiles on the dashboard, empty states, success confirmations, error illustrations. Used sparingly; static Lucide icons remain the default everywhere else.
animated.fullSetBody: There are 52 animations in total — search and preview the full set on the dedicated page.

accessibility.title: Accessibility
accessibility.description: Global accessibility rules every component must meet — keyboard, ARIA, contrast, motion, touch targets. The Colors, Motion, and Typography foundations carry these forward; this page is the source of truth.

keyboard.title: Keyboard
keyboard.intro: Every interactive element must be reachable, operable, and visible to keyboard users. "Mouse-only" is never acceptable — keyboard support is also the foundation for screen reader, voice, and switch-control access.

items.tabOrder: Tab order follows visual order. If your layout flexes content out of source order, fix it with tabindex carefully — most cases should be solved by markup order, not tabindex tricks.
items.focusRing: Focus rings must be visible and have at least 3:1 contrast against their surroundings. Components use the --ring token which is already tuned — don't override focus:outline-none without restoring a visible indicator.
items.shortcuts: Common keys are expected to work everywhere they apply: Escape closes overlays (dialogs, popovers, command palette), Enter/Space activates buttons, arrow keys navigate within radio/select/tab groups.
items.trap: Modal dialogs must trap focus while open — Tab cycles within the dialog, never escapes back to the page. The codebase uses Radix Dialog which handles this correctly; don't build your own.
items.restore: When an overlay closes, focus returns to the element that opened it. This is automatic with Radix; verify by opening any Dialog with Enter, closing with Escape, and confirming the trigger is focused again.

aria.title: ARIA
aria.intro: Prefer semantic HTML before reaching for ARIA — a real <button> beats <div role="button"> every time. Use ARIA to fill gaps in semantic markup, not to replace it.

items.semantic: Use the right element. <button> for buttons, <a> for navigation, <label> + form controls for inputs. Don't reinvent these as <div onClick> — you lose keyboard support, screen reader semantics, and form submission for free.
items.labelOrLabelledby: Every interactive element needs an accessible name. Visible text content is the default (<button>Save</button>). When the name isn't visible (icon-only button), use aria-label. When the name lives in another element, use aria-labelledby pointing at its id.
items.describedby: aria-describedby links to additional context — helper text below an input, an error message, a tooltip. Don't use it as a duplicate label; use it for the extra explanation.
items.current: Navigation items must declare which one is active. aria-current="page" on the current nav link is the standard pattern — visual highlight alone is invisible to screen readers.
items.live: Dynamic content updates (form errors, toast messages, search results) need aria-live so screen readers announce them. Use aria-live="polite" for non-urgent updates, "assertive" only for blocking errors.
items.divButton: Don't put role="button" on a div unless you also wire up Enter, Space, keyboard focus, and disabled state by hand. That's a lot of work for what <button type="button"> gives you free.

contrast.title: Color contrast
contrast.intro: WCAG defines minimum contrast ratios between foreground and background. The semantic tokens on the Colors page are pre-labeled with conservative AA/AAA chips — use them as a starting point, but verify per surface.

items.normal: Body text and small UI elements need 4.5:1 contrast against their background (WCAG AA). 7:1 hits AAA. text-foreground on bg-background already exceeds AAA in both modes.
items.large: Large text (18px+, or 14px+ bold) and non-essential UI (decorative icons, focus rings) only need 3:1. Several brand pairings — primary-foreground on primary, destructive-foreground on destructive — meet large but not normal-text AA. Use them on button labels, not paragraph copy.
items.nonText: Interactive boundaries (input borders, focus rings, button outlines) need 3:1 against adjacent colors. Disabled controls are exempt — their low contrast is the affordance.
items.tokens: The Colors foundation page tags each foreground token with its intended pair and a WCAG chip. Green = passes normal text. Amber = passes large only. Always honor the chip — don't put text-primary-foreground on bg-card and hope it works.
items.tools: Run axe DevTools in Chrome/Firefox for live audits. Browser devtools (Inspect → Accessibility → Contrast) checks a single pair. Lighthouse runs a holistic page check. The contrast chips on Colors page are conservative estimates — automated tools are authoritative.

motion.title: Motion
motion.intro: Motion can disorient or distress users with vestibular conditions. The Motion foundation page covers timing and easing; this page covers when motion is and isn't acceptable.

items.respect: Honor prefers-reduced-motion: reduce — the Motion page's override snippet must be included in your app stylesheet. Reducing means ~10ms (effectively instant), not zero — state changes still need to happen, just without animation.
items.notSoleSignal: Never rely on animation alone to signal state. A row that fades green on success is invisible to screen reader users — pair the fade with a text label, an icon change, or an aria-live message.
items.scroll: Don't hijack scroll. Smooth-scrolling page transitions are fine; locking scroll position or driving page content via scroll-jacking disorients users with motion sensitivity and prevents keyboard nav.
items.parallax: Parallax and ambient looping animations should be removed (not reduced) under prefers-reduced-motion. They add no information and actively harm vulnerable users.

touch.title: Touch targets
touch.intro: Touch targets must be large enough to hit reliably. WCAG 2.5.5 (AAA) requires 44×44 CSS pixels for primary interactive elements; 24×24 is the AA minimum for dense desktop UIs.

items.mobile: 44×44px minimum on mobile (WCAG AAA). The codebase's button sizes (xl=48, xxl=56) clear this comfortably; xs (32) and sm (36) do not — use only in desktop-dense contexts.
items.desktop: 24×24px minimum on desktop (WCAG AA). icon-xs (32) and icon-sm (36) sit safely above; the underlying interactive area must not shrink below 24×24 even when the icon itself is smaller.
items.spacing: Adjacent touch targets need 8px of clear space between them. Two buttons in a row should have gap-2 minimum — closer than that and users tap the wrong one on small screens.
items.iconButtons: Icon-only buttons collapse smaller than text buttons — be especially careful with sizing. A 24×24 icon needs at least size-9 (36px) of clickable area around it.

checklist.title: Pre-merge checklist
checklist.description: Run this before merging any component that adds new interactive behavior or visual state.

items.tab: Tab through the component start to finish — every interactive element receives focus in a sensible order, none are skipped, the focus ring is clearly visible.
items.screenReader: Read the component with VoiceOver (Mac) or NVDA (Windows). The role, label, and state should be announced; nothing is silent or surprising.
items.contrast: Run axe DevTools on the page. Resolve every contrast issue — automated tools catch what the human eye misses.
items.reducedMotion: Toggle prefers-reduced-motion in System Settings (Mac) or DevTools rendering tab. Animations should collapse to instant; nothing should still wobble.
items.keyboard: Operate the component with keyboard only. Open it, interact, close it, return focus. No mouse, no gestures.
items.labels: Every interactive element has an accessible name. Icon buttons have aria-label, inputs have <label htmlFor>, navigation has aria-current.
items.responsive: Component works at 320px width (smallest common phone). No horizontal scrolling, no clipped touch targets.
items.zoom: Browser zoom to 200%. Text reflows, nothing overlaps, no functionality is lost.
